225578983.review:
A legendary 10-year masterclass in tactical stress, "95% hit chances," and pure heartbreak.

It’s 2026, and XCOM 2 is officially ten years old, yet it remains the gold standard against which every other turn-based tactics game is measured. Firaxis took the "underdog" fantasy and dialed it to eleven, turning you from a global superpower into a ragtag resistance movement operating out of a stolen alien ship. The gameplay is a perfect, agonizing loop of high-stakes decision-making. Every time you send your squad into the field, you aren't just playing a game; you’re managing a group of people you’ve grown to love, knowing full well that a single bad roll or a poorly timed "Sectopod" reveal can result in permanent death. It is brutal, it is unfair, and it is arguably the most satisfying strategy experience ever put on Steam.

The "War of the Chosen" expansion is no longer just DLC; in 2026, it’s considered the only way to truly experience the game. The addition of the Chosen—mini-bosses who taunt you, kidnap your soldiers, and sabotage your research—adds a layer of personal rivalry that was missing from the base game. When you combine that with the three new resistance factions and the "Sitrep" mission modifiers, no two campaigns ever feel the same. This is a game where you spend half your time in the "Avenger" base management screen, sweating over which research project to prioritize, and the other half in the field, screaming at your monitor because your best Ranger missed a point-blank shotgun blast. It is a masterpiece of "calculated risk" that rewards brilliance and punishes overconfidence with equal enthusiasm.

What truly keeps XCOM 2 alive a decade later is the sheer insanity of its modding community. With the "Long War of the Chosen" mod and thousands of custom voice packs, maps, and classes, you can essentially play this game forever. Whether you’re turning your soldiers into characters from Star Wars or adding 500+ mods to create a "Season 10" hardcore campaign like the top YouTubers, the flexibility of the engine is legendary. While the industry has moved on to newer titles, most strategy fans eventually find themselves crawling back to the Avenger. It’s a game that respects your intelligence, tests your patience, and provides a sense of triumph that few other titles can match. Good luck out there, Commander—the Council is watching.

10/10 — I missed a 99% shot and lost my favorite medic. I’m going to uninstall, then immediately start a new Ironman run.

225081163.review:
XCOM 2 is honestly one of the most replayable strategy games I’ve played and easily one of my most played games with 350+ hours on it.

The gameplay loop is extremely satisfying. Building your squad, watching soldiers grow stronger over time, customizing their classes and loadouts, then risking everything in missions creates a really addictive cycle. The turn-based combat is tactical but also chill enough that you can play at your own pace, think through decisions, or even just casually play while doing something else on the side.

What makes the game nearly endless is the modding community. Mods completely transform the experience and can make every campaign feel different. From quality-of-life improvements to entirely new classes, enemies, mechanics, and progression systems, modding keeps the game fresh even after hundreds of hours.

One of the best recommendations is Long War 2 if you want a much longer and deeper campaign experience. It turns the game into a full-scale war simulator with more strategy, resource management, squad rotation, and overall complexity. If you enjoy RPG-style progression, slower pacing, and longer campaigns, it keeps the game spicy and highly replayable.

The downside is that the game can feel punishing, especially early on. Losing experienced soldiers hurts, mistakes snowball quickly, and RNG can sometimes make you question reality after missing a 95% shot. The learning curve is also much harder if you jump in blindly.

Overall, XCOM 2 is one of the best turn-based strategy games out there, especially if you enjoy tactical gameplay, progression systems, and games that can endlessly evolve through mods.

This is not for you if:

You dislike turn-based combat
You get frustrated easily with RNG or punishing gameplay
You prefer fast-paced action games
You don’t enjoy planning, positioning, and long-term squad management
You dislike repetitive gameplay loops or slower long-form progression

224826735.review:
This is one of the best turn-based strategy games I have ever played. It is also one of the hardest. The theme is very interesting. Earth has already lost the war. Aliens rule the world, and you lead a small resistance from a hidden base.
The difficulty is no joke. Every move counts. You will miss shots you thought were guaranteed hits. Your best soldiers can die forever because of one bad decision. Mission timers force you to take risks. The Avatar Project is always moving forward. The pressure never stops.
What I like about XCOM 2 is that you never know what will happen next. A simple task can turn into a disaster in two turns. 
TLDR; I would recommend the game to anyone who likes turn-based strategy games and doesn’t mind a steep learning curve.

[h2] Pros: [/h2]
- Hard but fair
- Adjustable difficulty
- Great progression system
- Interesting theme
- Great soundtrack
- High replayability and great mod support

[h2] Cons: [/h2]
- Steep learning curve

[h1] Overall rating: 9/10 [/h1]

224205160.review:
XCOM 2 is the kind of game where, no matter how many times you play it through, whether you win or lose a single mission or complete the entire campaign, you always learn from the outcomes, realising what you did wrong and using that knowledge to tackle the next mission or campaign. The perfectly balanced classes, weapons, items and tactical options, the variety of enemies and the tactics you can use against them in addition to completing the mission itself — not to mention the abilities and equipment of your soldiers — provide a challenge that can deliver a truly enjoyable experience at certain points. 

I bought the game in 2018 and jumped straight into the War of the Chosen DLC without experiencing the original game. After beating two campaigns on Veteran and Commander difficulty, I got carried away with the countless mods I subscribed to on the Workshop. Unfortunately, I haven’t been able to return to the original experience since. While I have played for 1,170 hours since writing this review, I admit that at least 30–40% of that time was spent in the Alternative Mod Launcher, mainly rewriting config files for various mods.

This is one of the few games that is not only an amazing creation at its core, but also a timeless masterpiece thanks to its user-friendliness and the community that brings further modifications to refresh the game.

224156899.review:
Overall I liked the game, but felt more like an expansion to xcom 1 rather than a sequel. If you liked xcom 1, you'll probably like this game too.
Note that I only played the base game. I intend to replay the game again with the DLC at some point and will probably update this review if my opinion changes enough.

To get more into the specifics:
I liked a lot of the rebalancing from xcom 1. The secondary weapons are all very cool and fit each class well. The addition of the melee system was very cool. I liked the new specialist class a lot, hacking and remote healing are very cool additions. I like that psi is its own class now, rather than regular classes being able to use psi abilities. I like the armor system, I think it brings an interesting texture to battles. A lot of the new enemies were interesting, and the returning enemy redesigns all worked well.

However, there were a lot of things I also disliked. My biggest complaint is how buggy the game was. For example: units not attacking the enemy I indicated, and enemy pathing ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ up and running around in circles. I also ran into a fun bug on the second to last level, where an enemy blew up my cover and the rubble blocked my unit from moving at all. Very cool. I also dislike how short the campaign is, my first run clocked in at about 30 hours on the dot (I don't remember if xcom 1 was this short as well). Tangentially; I was also fairly disappointed that there were only 2 weapon/armor tech upgrades, which were basically the same as xcom 1. Also the game ran surprisingly bad for a game from 2016. In the later levels especially lots of exploding cover and fire effects really tanked the game's performance, and I dipped all the way down to 30FPS at one point. My PC isn't top of the line or anything, but it should obliterate a game this old, and especially a strategy game which shouldn't need fancy fire graphics or physics simulations.
In general, I thought the game didn't evolve enough from xcom 1. You'll notice my list of likes are things that are slightly different than the systems in xcom 1. And most of my gripes were also present in xcom 1. I liked it more than I disliked it, but I'm not sure how much of that is this game's own merit, or if it's nostalgia for xcom 1.

224037092.review:
XCOM 2 is simultaneously the most fun and the most frustrating game you will ever play. The developers created a great concept and then turned the game over to a bunch of degenerate gamblers who don't understand percentages to finish writing the code. You will miss so many 99% shots that you'll start to question if you know what 99% means. Reinforcements will drop in 5 feet from all of your units on overwatch and you will watch in disbelief as they fire so wildly, it's a miracle no one shot themselves. In fact, if one of your soldiers were to get depressed and decide to end it one night, it would take them multiple shots to finish the job. You will need your entire squad to kill one of their guys and they will one-shot you with a critical hit, even though you were across the map, up on a roof, hunkered down behind full cover, through 3 walls and a floor. What even is the point of cover if the enemy doesn't care if they live or die? They just run over and stand right beside you, going full kamikaze mode without a care in the world.

And you're going to need some mods, because for some reason the devs thought that strict timers in every mission was the best approach in a game about positioning and tactics. So if you don't want to be forced to be constantly moving forward during a gunfight, you'll need the mod to remove the timer. And if you don't want to waste several minutes hitting the same button for every squad member, you'll need mods for Extract All and Overwatch All. 

If save scumming was not a thing already, it would have been invented with this game. Every turn, something will happen that will make you go, "Wait. What?" and reload. If there is an XCOM 3, I'm begging the devs to rework the cover and percentage system. No one behind full cover should ever be critically hit. No one standing right next to a person should miss them. If you're going to force me to extract within a certain number of moves, don't have enemy attacks that are unavoidable and incapacitate my soldiers for 4+ turns. Don't have RNG hit chances that are clearly not RNG. I reloaded a 98% shot chance about 15 times and it missed every time. Which means the game has already decided beforehand if I'm going to hit or not.

223954654.review:
XCOM 2 is a tactical turn-based RPG with additional strategy elements. It has incredible presentation, serviceable plot, an incredibly rich and deep gameplay, and tons of mods on top of that. I played it on Veteran with integrated DLCs and had no issues with any DLC content being too hard. 

One thing I must mention before the detailed review, however, is the technical state. The game leaves quite a lot to be desired: some actions leave the game "thinking" for 5-10 seconds before proceeding. In other cases, the game keeps "thinking" seemingly forever, while the sounds are still going on, so it's not a total freeze. Those endless "freezes" happened about 10 times, which is roughly each 5 hours, so not critical, but pretty annoying. 

DO NOT use Iron man in-game option, I don't think the game is stable enough for it.

Now, the details. Starting with presentation, it's very good. All the sounds are crisp and clear, and they don't get boring even after dozens of hours. Grenade explosions, rifle sounds, even basic unit quips are wonderfully implemented. Moreover, there is an option to diversify unit languages e.g. a German soldier would speak in German. I cannot vouch for accuracy, but it is a pretty neat feature. Alien language is appropriately alien and disturbing as well. 
The voice acting is also pretty good. As I mentioned, the plot is serviceable, so there isn't really any room for VAs to flex their muscles, but what we have is solid, no issues. 

Visually, the game looks pretty good for its age. I really enjoyed aliens' designs, many customisation options you have for your soldiers, and how the weapons/armour change as you upgrade them. 

The only thing I can complain about is a certain late-game alien. It looks extremely goofy and out of place, unfortunately. Doubly so because it has story relevance and is supposed to be very important for the narrative. I have zero idea why the devs thought its appearance would be appropriate for its role in the story. 

The gameplay is absolutely amazing. XCOM offers tons of options on both tactical and strategical levels, multiple different classes, and dozens of items. The game is punishing, but it feels largely fair (99% misses notwithstanding). Your missions can go right or wrong in hundreds of various ways, especially with Lost fighting against the aliens too.  I initially save scummed, but decided to only replay missions as I got better, and it made the experience more personal and engaging. I highly recommend embracing the RNG nature of the game and live with your mistakes; it's still very approachable and quite easy on Veteran. 

Strategic level is interesting too. It's not as action-packed as tactical battles, but it provides a very pleasant sense of progression and of slowly growing your network, pushing the aliens back bit by bit. Aliens, in their turn, fight back and make all sorts of issues for you. 

When it comes to the plot, there is nothing special nor surprising about it, but it doesn't detract from the experience whatsoever. The magic of XCOM, in my opinion, is not in the overarching plot, but in smaller stories and experiences you go through while playing. The actual narrative is just OK. 

To summarise, I highly recommend XCOM 2. Start with a lower difficulty and perhaps save scum initially to get a grasp of how the game works, then move to fewer saves. If you enjoy its gameplay loop, there is infinite replay potential with hundreds of mods, for instance LWOTC.

223918086.review:
If you're on the fence about buying XCOM 2, ask yourself if you named your stuffed animals as a kid. If the answer is yes, you probably will like XCOM 2 regardless of the setting or gameplay. This is because XCOM 2 has a great framework for bringing any character you can think of to life. 

The following systems lead to a growing attachment to your soldiers:
- Customize name, race, sex, voice, language, nationality, outfit, backstory, disposition, color, patterns, and even create custom named weapons
- Out of mission consequences such as death, injury, mental trauma, loss of belongings, promotions, nicknames
- Randomized classes seem like a negative at first but allow for creative character and squad building. Make ninjas, drone operators, snipers, cowboys, heavy weapons guy, and more. 
- Cinematic camera/kill cam
- Soldiers can be seen interacting with each other/going about their days in your base
- Posters, photo mode, soldier bonds if you are play the War of the Chosen Expansion

In addition, Enemies feel unique in design and function, and combat in general always has this aspect of random chance which turns the combat into a game of strategic risk management, which leads to a lot of surprises, both good and bad.  

To conclude: Buy this game if you were the type of kid to have massive lego battles, and the setting at a first glance piques your interest. There's a lot of dlc, but my recommendation is to play the Vanilla game first, and with light, non-gameplay affecting mods at most. Then once you finish that and feel you'd like some more I'd definitely pick up WOTC. Maybe the bundle with Shen's last gift if you want robo soldiers.

Honestly game causes so much frustration at higher difficulties with how anyone of value dies in one hit that it deserves the dislike. Doesn't matter how much you prep, enemies constantly score crits that either instant kill or 2 turn kill your guys, while your guys miss every single overwatch shot. I don't know if my copy is bugged but I have watched myself miss, no joke, 5 shots in a row with a 75% hit chance. An entire team. The original was all about the slow crawl and setting ambushes but in this, every ambush is a waste of time because nothing hits, just bring a ton of grenades for guaranteed damage. What's even more frustrating is having to restart the game and do the same dumb 3 missions in a row that are incredibly slow. I want to play at something higher than the 'intended' difficulty because it's too easy, but anything higher and it's just basically a giant dice roll on if you're wiped in one turn. Like no joke my entire team was killed by a lucky turret, then when I take control of it, it has a 50% hit chance, the odds are so low for something like that even though I know its 'fair' and definitely not 'weighted dice'. Also the lost target you at a ratio of 7:3 which you can tell when a huge horde spawns and they ignore the enemies just to swarm your guys, real fun trying to use zombies strategically, nah lets have the zombies be annoying. As you can tell its a frustrating game, if you want a perspective on how frustrating it is, I found battle brothers less frustrating because I felt strategic placement, planning, and control, had some degree of impact.
Edit: I turned it all around, it's alright

222698811.review: Still worth playing in the current day, especially since they never did make XCOM 3 (no, Chimera Squad doesn't count) despite sequel baiting in the ending cutscene. By no means perfect, and there are some basic quality of life mods that you should always install (I play with Evac All, Stop Wasting my Time, and Show Health Values), but there's really nothing else that scratches this exact itch besides XCOM: Enemy Unknown. There are tons of great mods, but even the vanilla experience is a lot of fun, even without War of the Chosen. If nothing else, it's really fun to make your friends in the game and try to keep them alive (especially on Ironman). There is no denying this game can be extremely punishing at times, but outside of the very early game where you have little control over your loadouts, the player usually has complete agency over whether they win a mission or not. This does usually require playing carefully to be consistent, but I think Ironman is by far the most rewarding way to play the game. Though I'm admittedly not an Ironman purist, and I will savescum turns occasionally (though ironman prevents anything more than this). Point being, if the game sounds like fun, give it a shot on sale, there's a good chance you'll enjoy it.

Preface: I might come back to try and muster up the patience to finish this game later, but I'm definitely done for now, and simply can't in good faith, recommend it to anyone that has to spend money on this, like I did. My faith in the franchise depends on me just stopping while I'm ahead.

After purchasing on a Steam sale in order to be able to actually play the game, I stuck with it for almost 40 hours, and I just can't go on. I LOVED Enemy Within. When it comes to that game, I can't get enough XCOM. I love the series. I even like the combat in 2. I liked the graphics in 2. (even though it is FULL of errors and glitches) The problems, bugs, mechanics, poor designs, design flaws, and antics however, just make 2 a huge loser for me, and from what I read, many others as well.

I got to about the halfway mark, and just had to throw in the towel. It's not too hard, it's just too stupid. Unless you are playing on Ironman, it is just a reroll simulator. Losing a game because you made a bad choice is one thing. Losing a game, because they designed enemies/attacks/stages/items, that do whatever they want, ignores the rules of combat COMPLETELY, and are FULL of bugs and glitches to boot, is something else entirely.

The game doesn't respect the players time, and has no regard for decency. The UI is one of the worst things I've ever dealt with, and I really hope they do better in XCOM 3. 2 was such a disaster, that there is now a HUGE need for "3". At least we still have Enemy Within to tide us over until another good one comes out.
hash tag - makexcomfunagain

222318941.review:
9 / 10
Finally finished the game, but on PS4 (I completed 41 missions and it must've took me at least 45 hours from beginning to end). Great strategy game, much better than its predecessor, it's one of the very best of its kind next to DIVINITY ORIGINAL SIN 2 and DRUIDSTONE.
I previously tried playing this on Commander difficulty here on Steam, I think I was 1/3 way through, but this game is renowned for being really hard in the beginning when you're limited with soldier numbers, abilities and resources. I found it very tough back then, I did not understand what I can or can't do with resource management stuff, and I then got distracted with another game at the time. For the past month, I really got back into it, I restarted the game on PS4, with first half of gameplay being on Veteran difficulty. On this difficulty, I got the better hang of it, the game started becoming too easy halfway through, so I upgraded to Commander difficulty for the rest of the game. The challenge raised a bit, and the last mission was quite tricky with numerous hordes of aliens around you, but I beaten it in the end. The increase of squad numbers and the mimic beacons really turned the tide.
On PS4, it is playable, though with visual bugs and lags (the game has randomised map layouts), but on Steam, the game is very well polished.

222049808.review:
I loved the original X-COM when it first came out.  Had the add-ons, terror from the deep and everything. Still have the original box somewhere.

These graphics are nice. And I still get drawn to it. But, after playing 5-15 hours, I invariably remember why I stopped.

As many have complained before, this is not a "fair game."  There are too many "coincidences" to be a coincidence.  And the they are skewed against the player. 

Yes, there are ways to resolve the imbalance.  But, they are tied to knowing what the quirks of the AI are rather than playing tactically based on how we know the real world works, or even how X-COM and its original spinoffs worked.  

Unable to target through floors as a player but being targeted through the floor by enemies.  Timer missions that require you to dash for the extraction point forcing you to pull a Leeroy Jenkins or at a minimum sacrificing one or more squad members. 

Even the simplification in customization, which means you end up with an equal distribution of each class. I can see why you might go out with Four or Six Rangers, or Heavy Weapons specialists.  But, why would anyone ever need a whole squad of Sharpshooters?

It's tough to hate a game.  Especially one with such a storied history.  But, this one just isn't for me.  For those of you able to tolerate or even somehow master this, good for you.  I just hope someone in charge of development reads these criticisms, which mostly seem to point to the some lack of rules, and fixes them if there is another major patch or version.

221895981.review:
In theory this game should be right up my alley. It has been on my wishlist for a long time until I recently picked it up in the Spring sale. Production quality is great and I feel invested, but it just doesn't respect the player's time or effort. Sure, you can tell me to 'git gud' or play on Easy or something, but the source of the difficulty isn't the issue - it's the mechanisms. I am careful to advance through cover and make sure my team cover each other - I'm not recklessly running in and trying to Rambo everything.
I'm not just talking about missing 95% shots twice in a row. I'm not talking about bad luck of having an enemy pod just decide to stroll up two ladders right into your hidden soldier on the roof. It's the combination of all of these things - making you feel like you have agency and you can apply strategy to achieve mission objectives, but in actuality it's just pure luck. Sorry, a Chosen assassin spawns to hamper your efforts. Cool, well she's on the other side of the map so I guess I have a few turns. No sorry, she's already next to your best unit and incapacitates them in one hit.
I don't like investing time in something to watch my team die to an unfair fight. It's not fun for anyone who isn't a masochist.

221866233.review:
The game is fantastic and definitely worth a purchase. Making the bad ending of the reboot canon was a brilliant move and made for a really unique XCOM game we hadn't had before.

So why is this a thumbs down? This negative review is not for the game itself, but for the Mac version of the game. The Mac version is out of date and runs horribly, and Feral has not updated it. If you're on Apple Silicon, forget it, just play something else or play this game on Windows or Linux, because the Mac version is severely unoptimized for the ARM chips. The same chips that run Cyberpunk like a dream can't handle XCOM 2 on anything above 30FPS on low settings, even if you're using the Max revisions that are specifically designed to have absurdly strong GPU performance.

If you're gonna play the game on Mac, play it using CrossOver, not the native Mac version, because the native Mac version just ain't up to snuff.

This game is absolute ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥. Every shot you take is a coinflip, probability is a lie. You will miss a point blank 99% shot, but the yolo 15% shot you take will hit and kill. Still not too sure if XCom was recruiting Storm Troopers or not.

The aliens are also complete jerks. Sectopods are an actual crime on humanity itself, I hope to god you got bluescreen rounds by the time this game throws multiple at you at a time. Sometimes aliens will appear out of nowhere and screw your player phase up. It's almost unfair how out of control this game is.

This game is a massive test of patience. You will be pissed off, you will be angry, but at least you can be glad your terrible luck is not going towards financial ruin. And I love it. I only have as many hours as I do because I'm busy with college and I know I will get addicted if I even so much as touch this game.

Every choice feels like it matters, that's what makes XCom feel more than a strategy game, it is a war game. The fate of your soldiers, how easy the rest of the game will be, the fate of the planet, everything depends on your choices, and I love it. It makes me feel more invested towards building a strong cast of soldiers, and the reward feels just as great. 

There are obviously some complaints I have. It's frusterating when a whole horde of enemies just appear out of nowhere when you try to advance, the game can be unoptimized at times, and modding the game itself is just hell. All of the amazing mods you can use make this game worth it alone. But getting them all to run is a nightmare, especially because the offical launchers are trash, always use the alternative one made by fans, trust me. 

It is 100% not going to be a game for everyone. If you're a casual gamer that does not like it when a game throws unfair BS at you, this game will be hard to get through. But it also has to be, you're leading a resistance that wants to take earth back from the aliens. Don't feel ashamed by save scumming, we have all done it, and it does not fix all your problems, it still leaves a puzzle you have to figure out when it rerolls events.

Easily recommend this to anyone who loves strategy games like myself. The game usually goes on sale for dirt cheap, so nab it and War of The Chosen if you can. Although I reccomend playing vanilla before War of the Chosen first. Both are very fun.

Also relist all the DLC seperatly again 2K, come on.

221788222.review:
Oof, well X-COM 2 is quite a wild ride, both if you’re new to series or came after first game, first meaning new X-COM, not old ones.

So X-COM 2 is turn based tactical strategy game separated into two parts - base management and combat. Base one is similar to first game, with one big difference - you build up yours not underground or even in cities, like on olden times, but aboard of big airship that is also used to travel around the world. So as you could guess, space is quite limited, got to plan ahead. Besides filling bowels of aircraft, you also do all other management things there - research and development, production of gear, training, recruitment, planing infiltration missions, communication with resistance etc. Basically you do everything to plan and prepare for eventual combat missions. Now combat itself is at core same as before - you move your team in turn based mode, flank and ambush enemies to get best chance at hitting them and miss, cause 99% hit chance is still actually 50% - you either hit or miss. That chance based hit system is pretty infamous and memed a lot. Compared to earlier title you get more enemy types, also half of them are new, they got more abilities, but so do you, there are some new useful skills to play with. Also War of the Chosen adds a bunch more enemies, including Chosen themselves, who are kinda midbosses and are more enjoyable than actual final confrontation. Besides new foes to fell, it gives three very distinct resistance factions which allow you to recruit their members who have great unique skills. And with those come new base mechanics for both aliens and you and new objectives for campaign. All in all War of the Chosen elevated very good game to being great one.

The plot continues after first game but starts already with plot twist - after your victory in first X-COM, aliens came back in force and Earth lost. Unlike before, where you start mission and get ambushed, this time more often than not, you’re launching in concealed state and lose effect of surprise only after being spotted or engaged. There are no longer any governments supporting you, only remaining resistance cells are giving help, but they need some assistance in return too. There is massive propaganda campaign that ensured good chunk of human population living happily under invaders rule with every shady stuff possible going on unnoticed. So for most part of game you are essentially a fugitive and every mission you take ends up in news twisted as terror attack. Ofc that’s just the beginning and more you go on through campaign progression more you learn of what’s going on really. Once again War of the Chosen brought a side campaign sorta that goes along with main one and is just as entertaining to experience, including new resistance factions.

Graphically XCOM 2 is slightly better looking than first one, not that much of great improvement there. In general game looks decent, not cutting edge, but definitely above average with occasional outstanding action shots sometimes deserving close-up cam that shows them in all their glory. There are some not that great looking textures stretching on some units rarely, but well, it’s been 10 years now and this is not exactly first or third person genre, extreme close-ups are not main focus here camera-wise.

Audio is pretty good, probably better quality than Enemy Within in ost department, cause ambient and gameplay noise was already without any complaints great. It’s not like any new music was added to my playlist, but sometimes you notice how good track is while playing, which stands out from usual background role such stuff takes in strategies.

Another aspect that was already mentioned above needs another shoutout is War of the Chosen expansion - it’s really great stuff and while not essential for game, it enhances experience greatly adding pretty good amount of new stuff, so better get it from the start.

220858489.review:
[h1]XCOM 2[/h1]

[b]XCOM 2[/b] is a tactical strategy game where humanity already lost the war against aliens… and now it’s your job to fix that little problem. You lead the resistance, manage a secret mobile base, recruit soldiers, research alien tech, and send squads on extremely dangerous missions where one bad decision can turn your favorite sniper into alien lunch.

If you like strategy, planning, and screaming at your screen when a soldier misses a 95% shot, welcome to your new addiction.

Every mission feels like a high-stakes chess match with lasers, grenades, and occasionally a giant alien monster ruining your perfectly planned strategy.

[b]Why it’s amazing:[/b]
• Deep tactical gameplay: Every move matters, and positioning can mean life or death.
• Permadeath soldiers: When a soldier dies, they’re gone forever… which makes every mission tense.
• Huge customization: You can fully customize your soldiers and grow attached to them (which makes losing them hurt even more).
• Base management: Research alien tech, upgrade weapons, and manage the resistance.
• Massive mod support: The Steam Workshop adds tons of content, missions, and gameplay improvements.

[b]Some alien-related frustrations:[/b]
• RNG can be brutal: Missing a 90%+ shot will absolutely happen at the worst moment.
• Difficulty spikes: Some missions can suddenly become extremely tough.
• Losing soldiers hurts… emotionally and strategically.
• The strategy layer can feel overwhelming for new players.

[u]Overall:[/u]
XCOM 2 is one of the best tactical strategy games ever made. The mix of turn-based combat, base management, and high-risk decisions creates constant tension and incredibly satisfying victories.

[b]Final verdict:[/b]
A brilliant strategy game where every mission feels like a desperate fight for survival — and where a 95% shot somehow still misses at the worst possible time.

220380288.review:
An amazing strategy game that I've played through multiple times and will play through again. The tactics game played within each level is simple to understand but the amount of depth is revealed the more you understand the game and the more items/abilities you unlock. The base building aspect played between each level is just as engaging with every decision (especially in the early game) feeling important for making it through the campaign.

The game is best played on the higher difficulties where it becomes very difficult but in the best way. There’s no repeating fights over and over to eventually beat them through pattern recognition, just constant new scenarios where your performance in each affects the next. Through the campaign the game progresses and changes to give you a wider range of tactics to use without bloating into a micromanagement nightmare like a lot of other strategy games can. The theme of this game is beautifully executed, you're always in the dark about what aliens you’ve never seen before can do and the game never fails to surprise you.The WOTC DLC is a must add which improves on the game even more making it the great game that it is.

219562173.review:
TL;DR: Got tired of learning game mechanics through trial and error.

If I lie in wait around a corner, and an alien rounds that corner, who gets to act first? The "overwatch (concealed)" unit? Wrong, it's the alien, unit is dead.
Surprise, melee enemy has spawned and hit your unit (this type of spawn was foreshadowed in a cutscene, which would be very clever in a movie and not a tactics game). You should now retreat, but how far can that enemy move? You have to guess. Guessed too low, unit is dead.
How long will this effect last on this unit? Guessed too high, and lost a guy trying to save his comrade who was fine next turn anyway.
What's the penalty for failing a mission? Voiceline after I'd cleared the main objective and lost many guys said that failing to kill all enemies just means a supply penalty. Wish I'd known that immediately after clearing the main objective, wouldn't have fought that many guys. Time to save-scum that mission.
Later on I apply that knowledge and rescue civilians, taking moderate casualties. I don't even try to kill all the enemies. It's bruising, but surely leaving the civilians to die is bad. Turns out, no, it counts as a flat failure because I didn't kill all the enemies. The penalty? Less supplies. Wish I'd known that, would have left them to die and spared my guys.
You can't even know if a certain position has line-of-sight to an enemy without quicksaving, trying it, and quick-loading if it doesn't work.

It's still so fun regardless that I was going to take all that as a learning experience, and start my campaign over. Thinking about it, I should have skipped more missions and let more research finish. But wait, aside from supply penalties, why not just let *all* the research finish, and *all* the guys heal up, before my next engagement? And I realized that there's almost certainly a mechanic to penalize that, but I won't know what it is until they introduce it, because they want the overworld to play like a movie and only drip-feed me mechanics. I'm not willing to burn another campaign run figuring that out.

219071738.review:
[h1] A Genuine Review [/h1]

[h3] Gameplay [/h3]

I went into this expecting a traditional turn-based experience — and from everything I’d heard, this is thee turn-based game. Coming from titles like Fire Emblem, Darkest Dungeon, and Mewgenics, I thought I had a pretty solid idea of what I was getting into. The best way I can describe this game — and I kind of hate using this terminology — is that it feels like the Dark Souls of turn-based games. It heavily punishes mistakes, which is fine in theory. But it also expects you to know things in advance that you realistically wouldn’t know without trial-and-error or save-scumming. And that’s where the frustration kicks in. In an action game, if something unexpected happens, you can react. You can dodge, reposition, adjust on the fly. In a turn-based game, you can’t. If something completely out of left field happens and it’s not your turn, you just have to sit there and watch it unfold. There’s no reaction — only consequence. Now, normally that wouldn’t be a dealbreaker. You die, you restart, you apply what you learned, and you do better next time. That’s a satisfying loop. But here’s the twist: when your soldier dies, they’re permanently gone. So imagine you’re told to complete an objective. You follow the instructions. It turns out that was the worst possible move. Your unit dies. Permanently.

What now?

If you don’t save-scum, you’ve just lost a character you invested time into — potentially because the game withheld critical information. If you do save-scum, it’s not challenging — it’s just tedious. And to make it worse, when the game tells you something is a good idea… don’t trust it. Especially as a new player still learning the systems. Following advice at face value can easily get your squad killed. Also, to the people yelling “skill issue”, I’m fine with difficulty. I’m fine with consequences. But difficulty should come from meaningful decisions and clear information — not from hidden gotchas that you only learn about after losing something permanently. 

[h3] Controversy [/h3]

Another major reason I’m refunding the game is the updated EULA.

I bought this a while back and had been meaning to give it a proper shot. But after the changes to the software license agreement, I’m just not comfortable keeping it installed. I don’t want more spyware sitting on my computer — especially not attached to a game I didn’t end up enjoying in the first place.

At least I gave it a fair try, and it’s simply not for me. But the EULA changes are a separate issue. That’s not a “me problem” — it’s a company pushing terms that many players won’t question or push back against. I’d rather draw the line and opt out. Luckily I did myself a favor and didn't force myself to play more of this game, so I can actually refund it. It may be popular to some, but it isn't purfect.

218711961.review: XCOM 2 is one of the most intense and rewarding strategy games I've ever played — the combination of tactical turn-based combat and base management creates constant tension that keeps you glued to your seat. The soldier customization is deep enough that you genuinely mourn the permadeath of your carefully built veterans, which makes every mission feel genuinely high-stakes. The modding scene is absolutely incredible, and honestly transforms an already great game into something with near-unlimited replayability — I went from casual fan to obsessive after loading up a Star Wars mod collection. Just know going in that the RNG will betray you at the worst possible moments, because apparently 95% hit chance means something very different in XCOM than it does in math class. Despite the occasional crash and the infuriating dice rolls, I can't recommend this game enough — grab it with the War of the Chosen DLC and a mod manager and you're set for hundreds of hours.

218605457.review:
When you consider that Majority of players have lost their first campaign in the XCOM: Enemy Unknown/Within. It understandable why XCOM 2 is set in Alien Occupation of Earth. Although I'd prefer the Original ending for XCOM in 1994... The Alien exterminate the Human Race. Destroying everything that left no traces of Humanity's previous life. 
So we have this concept in where Humanity is under control of Advent (Basically the current government and rulers of the Earth). Humanity as of the current story indicates is that Humans believe that the Aliens have benefited their lives. However, You the commander of XCOM, A Resistance organization now, must discover the truth of the Alien's intent, engage both the aliens that will attempt to stop you, Expose the truth, and Free Earth from the Alien's Control. 

Simple? No...
Unless you're playing on the lowest difficulty, You're going to learn it hard that the Aliens don't hold back their punches. Each enemy poses new threats and abilities that will test your cunning and luck for the fight. Every choice has benefits and ramifications that varies. Let it be know that unless you save-scum, You're going to lose Soldiers. Embrace it. As you progress, you'll learn from those actions and become battle-hardened. Or have all you plans fall to chaos when your soldier misses a 95% Chance to hit. But that XCOM, Baby. 
But time is against you. You don't have the luxury of turtling every mission or wasting time. The Aliens are moving against you that if they're given the chance to develop their "Project", XCOM fails and you lose the game. So it's important to keep making progress to stop their agenda.

Tips and Tricks
- Walls and Floors can be Destroyed. So beware when you place your Soldiers on rooftops. God forbid the enemy throws a grenade or blows that roof up and sends your units crashing down hard. You can find clips on youtube of players relying too hard on rooftops with floors capable of being destroyed.

- Never EVER, DASH into the FOG OF WAR. If your units aren't in concealment (Hidden Mechanic enabling you to ambush patrols), god forbid they get spotted. The enemy will get a free move and will take advantage of it. Resulting in them flanking your units and allowing them to get into an advantage. I know this game has a Time limit on missions, but the last thing you want is losing soldiers. 

- Research Magnetic Weapons and Plated Amour (Tier 2 Weapons and Armour). The Alien + Advent will keep getting stronger and Stronger, Eventually, you're not dealing enough damage to them to the point where you're using up your entire squad's turn to kill one alien out of the god knows how many have spotted you. Unless you take pleasure in Risking your soldiers getting One shotted.  

- [WOTC Expansion] KILL THE CHOSEN AS SOON AS POSSIBLE. THEY'LL KEEP GETTING STRONGER AND BECOME MORE AND MORE DIFFICULT WHEN THEY SPAWN IN YOUR MISSIONS. 

- [Alien Hunters] If you have to fight both these guys and Chosen..... Good f--king Luck, They're going to hurt you... So much.

218364744.review:
XCOM 2 is one of the most intense and rewarding strategy games ever created. It perfectly blends deep tactical combat with meaningful strategic management, keeping you constantly engaged and thinking several moves ahead.

Every mission feels high-stakes, thanks to the excellent turn-based mechanics and the permanent consequences of your decisions. The customization options for soldiers add a personal touch, making every victory thrilling and every loss truly emotional.

The atmosphere is gripping, with a strong sense of urgency as humanity fights back against alien occupation. Combined with smart level design and varied mission objectives, the gameplay never feels repetitive.

Overall, XCOM 2 is a masterclass in tactical strategy — challenging, immersive, and incredibly satisfying.

Não sei como tem tanta avaliação positiva.
O jogo é feito pra ser frustrante.

Você ta o tempo todo correndo contra o tempo, em um jogo que gastar tempo é uma das mecânicas principais.

Precisa de intel ou de recurso? Usa 5 dias. 
Clicou errado no mapa, perdeu mais 1 dia.
Se gastar tempo demais, perde o jogo geral.

Em cada missão tem outro problema, os inimigos são extremamente roubados. 
Seu personagem começa com 4 de vida, os inimigos tbm. Eles dao 5 de dano, você 3. E a chance dos seus personagens errarem é muito maior que de os inimigos errarem, nao importa se você ta bem escondido e etc.

Ai conforme voce avança, seus personagens evoluem. Os vilões também evoluem. Mas continuam dando hit kill, e advinha? Seu personagem morreu? Ja era.
Vai ter que evoluir outro dnv.

Ah mas ai seria so jogar umas missões de baixo nivel pra evoluir o personagem novo ne? Poise, nao tem, voce vai aparecer com um personagem lvl 1-2 no max, numa missão com os vilões muito mais fortes.

Um jogo de estrategia ter hitkill do inimigo é surreal. Não tem como reviver se morreu. A cura é escassa e cura pouco.

O design desse jogo é completamente insano.

Ah, colocar no facil nao adianta, continuam dando hit kill, so diminui a quantidade de inimigo.

Gastei um tempão nessa jossa atoa.
Perdeu? Azar o seu.

This one is poorly optimized, buggy as all get out, and imbalanced. It is also extremely fun.
It's fundamentally MEANT to be a different experience from the previous installment, and I ENJOY IT for that fact. They are different games meant for different types of strategy. Chimera squad was meant to be the same, but I would save that for the chimera squad review.

This game NEEDS mods for QOL and proper functioning, not even getting into "adding fun factor" which they do wonderfully as well. It's in the similar vein to Fallout 4 where the mods are the main reason to get the game.

I LOVED X-COM 2, and it holds a special place in my heart. But with how saturated and cash grabby the gaming industry has become, I feel the need to reflect in a more discerning manner. Hoping game companies that did a good job in the past can continue to do such.

X-COM 2 needed more time in the oven, but what it does well makes the game ENTIRELY a worthwhile experience, if not for the price. Right now it's on a 95% sale, and that's amazing for the quality of the game. 

I'd happily pay the full price if it included all the DLC- but 2.99 for the base game is EASILY a deal I'd take 9/10 times. This is the 1 instance where I bought it already.

If you can get it on sale, even 25% is a good deal for the base game. I just lament that it was part of the growing wave of DLC to finish games that felt incomplete without it. (Although, not NEARLY to the extent of games like Stellaris. But it's not about "whose the worst"- I care for the practices to squeeze money out of us in a way that feels exploitative. This skirts the line enough to call out as an example of hindsight.)

It was an omen of what was to come, for the ENTIRE gaming industry, and I hope Firaxis learns from ALL of their previous attempts (yes, even chimera squad. There were interesting ideas there, even if the execution of was lacking- failure is just another way to learn) as well the original "XCOM" series.

They've shown so much talent and competence when making their games, but it's a matter of if they are given the time and resources to do such. I hope whatever game they make next, will at least remind me why I didn't mind the jank in this game.

Heck, imagine a game where you can play AS the Ethereals (or just a similar role), if they just wanted to do another "experimental" game. I want these devs to push themselves. Maybe smaller titles to experiment while they push out slower, but more refined big titles. But hey, I don't know game dev- so maybe I should just be satisfied that we got what we did.

217953962.review: Out of the 20-30 Tactical RPGs I have played. This one is by far the worst one amoung them. If you play on the Standard Difficulty of the Base XCOM 2. You'll have a good time. As soon as you add "The War of the Chosen" which adds 3 Mini Boss that act as regional boss that invade missions you play on, on top of the various penalties you have playing on Commander/Legend. Your playing odds that are not in your favour. This is fine until you learn of the game's built in "Pre-determined outcome sequences". Say your unit will die if they can't deal 4 damage to an enemy with 4 HP. Your damage rolls are 3-4. You have 99% Accuracy. You character miss. You reload a save. Same outcome you miss. You will miss every, single, time . If you do another action then attack, the outcome will be different despite having the same odds of hitting and damage rolls. This means that game decides whether you will just outright lose an encounter or not before you've even come up with strategy to counter it, if you do not utilize save scumming to alter action orders between your team. Save Scumming is normally pathetic but, in this game. It's a core gameplay feature. Combine that with the degree of Time/Resource Management where if you fail to get strong enough or obtain better gear by "x" event. You can soft lock yourself if you haven't been piling up multiple saves at different points because you were save scumming. Game plays more like an RTS than a Tact.RPG with that level of Time/Resource management. At least it only cost $10 on sale...

217130423.review: I was new to the XCOM franchise so this was my first experience and only played the base game.  I enjoyed the military theme and aesthetic.  The SFX sound great and provides a more immersive experience to the world of XCOM, especially the voice acting.  The game does a great job of providing you context and background while also moving the plot forward in a way that doesn't bore you.  With that said, there's minimal downtime between combat (or missions).  As a CRPG veteran XCOM2 will challenge you, sometimes frustratingly so especially in the early game when you're learning the nuances and strategies.  Which segues into some of my dislikes about this game.  I'll preface my first dislike by pointing out each squad member has a hit percentage chance based on weapon type and distance to the target.  However, through my own experience I noticed the game predetermines if you're going to hit a target or not (damage seemed very RNG).  I noticed several times that regardless if you have +90% chance of hitting the target you WILL MISS based on the game's predeterminations. I have tested my own theory multiple times and have certainly confirmed my suspicions on this.  My workaround to this BS shadow mechanic was selecting another squad member to eliminate the target.  My only other dislike was the UI was clunky at times because it appears the game was initially made for console players.   Overall, I enjoyed this game and can certainly see why it's received so many awards and praise throughout the years.  Highly recommend to anyone interested that enjoy CRPGs and strategy games.

217046839.review:
XCOM 2 is one of those rare games that pulls you in and doesn’t let go. From the very first mission, it hits that perfect balance of tension, strategy, and storytelling. You’re constantly outnumbered, often outgunned, but every victory feels earned — and that’s exactly what makes it so rewarding.

The turn-based combat is brilliantly designed. Every move matters, and success depends on carefully weighing the risks of each decision. The procedural map generation keeps missions fresh, and the variety of enemies ensures no encounter feels routine. Losing a veteran soldier genuinely stings, which makes your squad’s survival all the more meaningful.

Visually, the game’s dark, rebellious atmosphere fits perfectly with the story of a resistance force fighting back against an alien occupation. The base management between battles adds a satisfying strategic layer — upgrading weapons, researching alien tech, and choosing which missions to tackle next all tie beautifully together.

Even years after its release, XCOM 2 hasn’t lost any of its edge. With its deep tactical gameplay, gripping tension, and endless replayability, it remains one of the best strategy experiences out there. If you love games that challenge your mind and make every decision count, XCOM 2 deserves a spot at the top of your list.

216736217.review:
XCOM 2 — The turn-based tactics gold standard
Rating: ★★★★★ | Verdict: Recommended

If you love turn-based strategy, this is as good as it gets. The combat + base/management layer is the real hook, and the “you already lost the war” resistance setting is a great twist that keeps the tone strong.

Pros
- One of the best games out there for turn-based tactics fans
- Great premise: you’re the resistance after losing the war, fighting to take ground back
- Combat is excellent and genuinely challenging on higher difficulties (in a good way)
- The strategy/management layer is phenomenal and adds real weight to every decision
- Strong customization makes you care about your soldiers (loss actually hurts)
- War of the Chosen is basically required, massive DLC and worth every penny
- Modding scene is huge and adds endless replayability
- Graphics still hold up surprisingly well

Cons
- Story is just “fine” you’re here for tactics/management, not narrative
- RNG shots will hurt your feelings sometimes (it’s part of the experience and not fully a con TBH)
- Performance is great vanilla/DLC; mods are fine too, but it depends how crazy you go
- The fact we may never get XCOM 3 is depressing

Best for: Anyone who wants deep turn-based combat, meaningful strategy-layer decisions, and high replayability.
Skip if: You hate permadeath, don’t like missing “high %” shots, or want story-first games.

Bottom line: With War of the Chosen, this is an all-time great. A must-play for tactics fans.

216342833.review:
Stylish, accessible and fun. While the early game is intense and brutal, the difficulty curve smoothens out when the second tier of weapon and armor upgrades are unlocked. I thoroughly enjoyed thinking about where to position my troops to maximize their hit chance and minimize damage taken. The main tactical grid-based gameplay has variety and is designed well.

Apart from a few challenging scenarios, enemies do not outscale XCOM troops and it is easy to one-shot weaker aliens with a bit of thinking. The power fantasy is there for players who want to feel rewarded for their perserverance.

That said, however, the late game could use a little more tension as I found myself forgoing the final tech upgrades after the main story upgrades were completed. There was a lot of waiting time to complete each final upgrade, so I went ahead with the final mission instead.

The management layer also lacks strategy since planning for facilities and radio relays in advance is generally not needed to succeed. The economy revolves around intel, alien alloys and elerium cores rather than supplies which are an easily acquired resource, and I wish the game gives players more options and challenges to acquire those.

All in all, an enjoyable vanilla experience. Now onto the Long War mod!

215597921.review:
This game has *ISSUES*
First off, the writing is terrible. Coming from the first game, it looses all sense of seriousness and turns into a marvel level qipps every 5 seconds. It is utterly insufferable especially with certain missions and characters. I kid you not there is a mission where the characters chirp back and forth with "quircky" dialouge almost entirely without pause for what is easily an hour long plus mission. (it involves robots)

Secondly, this games balancing is bad. Not "I struggled therefor game suk" its more like the game really enjoys killing your soilders without warning and often from full health. The snake enemies have a long range grapple that pulls characters in and also stuns them and almost never misses even through walls which is particuarly obnoxious especially when they pull someone completely out of line of sight of everyone else. It's compounded by a very small turn limit on most missions that effectively results in a squad wipe if you dont get everyone out in time which necessitates moving fast reguarly just killing your pointman. It makes sense why it was done, to prevent overwatch camping that was so common in the first game but it just doesnt mesh with the rest of the mechanics. There are also boss enemies that like to spawn in the middle of missions at random and get a turn every time you act, including movement that also just love to kill your frontliners before you can react and trying to have them retreat just gives it a free turn.

Its all the most frustrating because there is still a great core of a game there and from what I read from others the game can really shine with mods but my experience with a vanilla playthrough was awful. I haven't gotten this angry playing a game since I played multiplayer shooters like overwatch. 
Lastly is the price, 60$ for a 10 year old game with this many issues and quite a few bugs? This game doesnt deserve 20 dollars, let alone 60. 
If you really wana play it, buy it on sale (I got it for 5$) and download a modpack before you bother with the campaign.

215568626.review:
Before I start, this game was built in the same company that made civilisation, meaning its addictiveness is beyond control.

This game is as punishing as possible for a turn-based game (only matched by Darkest Dungeon), but thats where its fun comes from.

Defensive crawling is removed, and your pretty much only choice is the offensive.  Winning battles does not mean war however, and you need to have a strong economy to win the war.  Essentially, this game is about constant snowballing until all is over.  Most commanders fail to achieve this.

However, if you ever manage to get the snowball ongoing and overthrow the aliens, you will now realise why this game is popular.

215444642.review:
Love this game overall

Pros
- challenging game at legend difficulty, especially mid-game and strategy layer planning
- lots of different enemies, generated maps
- tons of customization options
- mod support, which makes certain gameplay more convenient, like Gotcha Again and Legacy Speedup

Cons
- some gameplay mods features should come with the game
- default zip mode is not adjustable, so without Legacy Speedup (which doesn't work with WOTC), gameplay is too  slow because of prolonged combat animations
- specialist class is underwhelming in combat compared to grenadier and sniper class
- probably the biggest complaint is the "best" and probably only strategy being not give aliens a turn, so even enemies are supposed to bring different challenges into combat, their skills don't matter since late mid-game, because we don't get a chance to see them following the best strategy. So they are essentially the same to combat strategically, only difference is their hit points and defense.
- generated maps look similar after a long campaign and becomes boring. and combat, as mentioned above, becomes monotonous: use phantom ranger to scout, place your soldiers just outside of next pod of enemies' line of sight, and sniper squadsight overwatch to activate the pod.

214852552.review:
This entire game is just artificial difficulty. It's a strategy game that's very heavily reliant on RNG, making strategy basically null. This is especially true in the early game where chances to hit are insanely low. Because of this It doesn't feel fun or satisfying when you actually complete a mission successfully, it just feels like you got lucky. It's just an endless series of saving/loading and trial and error. 

I see a lot people responding to comments and defending it claiming that it's just a difficult game, but I feel like this game is difficult in the same way that a slot machine is difficult. It's not a matter of intelligence or skill, it's a matter of whether or not a series of numbers you have very little actual control over line up. You can set up your team and formulate a great strategy just to get bad rolls 4 times in a row and lose the entire squad. 

This wouldn't be a huge issue if the game wasn't extremely time consuming as well. When the RNG decides you lose, you lose a large investment of time. There are times where you get into a bad situation and you either have to completely restart the mission or jump 5 saves back and lose 20 - 30 minutes of work. 

 
Fun game if you enjoy wasting your time and having your work destroyed by a random dice roll.

I played the first XCom a decent amount on my console and the changes between the first game and this one was significant. It ended up better, thankfully, and I can say XCom 2 is a rare situation where the developer takes an idea and expands it in the right ways.

Base-building is back and better fleshed out, squad actions are more meaningful while offering a few different ways to tackle objectives, and the grand scale of the overworld adds a bit more urgency and flavor to the mix.

My one nitpick is the DLC. When I first started playing this game with War of the Chosen I always stopped when you hit the first DLC boss and it took me way too long to figure out how to beat him. When I overcame that hill, however, I found myself enjoying the game just as much as before and eventually completed back-to-back playthroughs in short order.

Give it a shot if you enjoyed the first game.

214399060.review:
This is the best game of it's genre.  The gameplay is spotless, the risk and reward in every move, the strategy involved in very single battle feels incredible.  The classes feel well balanced and each has its own pros and cons that add to the gameplay.  I often found myself getting lost for 10 minutes just thinking about what to move next, contemplating the safest or the most aggressive move, which strategy might be best, or where I might find a hidden enemy.  Characters grow as the game progresses and sometimes the cost of their lives has to be considered, do you sacrifice a rookie or let your high level vet sniper get gunned down?   The decisions this game forces on you are thought provoking and incredibly fun, plus the insane amount of variability in every single level is staggering, making the replayability of this game unmatched for its genre.  Everything about it feels well thought out, well balanced.  I keep coming back to replay the campaign, or pick up the DLC on sale, dropping a 10-20 hours again and again.
5/5

Stay focused, save often and you will prevail! More entertainment in one package than a dozen AAA games. Enjoy!!!

214363317.review:
At first, I didn't like the premise of XCOM 2 because it made the bad ending of XCOM 1 canon. No longer are you leading a well oiled military machine funded by the entire world to combat the alien threat. You're no commander at the helm of earth's elite but a recently rescued P.O.W. leading some bare-bones group of wanna-be alien killers. You've failed horribly in the past and now Xenu Flirbnark over there owns both your backyard, your data and your genes. He's in your genes, man. 

But surprise surpise it's actually super great. I played War of the Chosen multiple times but didn't care much for the story apart from the "let's kick E.T.'s Butt".The tactical map gameplay and customization are where it's at, especially with mods. It can be incredibly tense, frustrating and satisfying. Many players will tell you to "git gud". These are actual IRL Aliens who want to enrage you into mistakes. Don't fall for them, no matter the game. The real tip, from human to human, is learn how to lose. Always.

I always grew really attached to my soldiers, guerilas and other dweebs, but I could never get into the story-characters. Sure, f*ckin' "punished" Bradford and the gang are all fine, but they pale in comparison to the adventures of knock-off Australian Freddy Mercury and the time he almost killed my best sniper when I made him throw a grenade at a glass roof.

It's really good and you should get it if you liked XCOM: Enemey Unknown or maybe even if you liked the more recent CIV games.

213425160.review:
XCOM 2 is brutal, unpredictable, and completely addictive. Every mission feels like a desperate gamble where one bad move can cost you a veteran soldier you’ve grown weirdly attached to. The turn-based combat is tense and tactical, with loads of meaningful choices in how you position, flank, and use abilities – and when the plan actually works, it’s incredibly satisfying.

The meta-layer on the Avenger, managing research, upgrades, resistance contacts, and the ticking doom clock, adds a constant sense of pressure that fits the “guerrilla war against aliens” theme perfectly. It can be frustrating at times (yes, the famous missed shots still hurt), but that’s also part of the charm: victories feel earned, losses sting, and every campaign tells its own story.

If you enjoy tactical strategy with real stakes, squad-building, and that constant “just one more mission” feeling, XCOM 2 delivers in a big way.

XComm 2(The continuation of XCOM: ENEMY UNKNOWN, which I'm currently doing a playthrough of...after XCOM 2, weird.. ik) is extremely surprising. For a base price of about 60$, though usually on sale for....10th of that, it's actually surprisingly VERY VERY addictive. Without spoiling too much, the game is relatively easy to get the hang of, but actually improving and getting good at it, takes significant time.

The positive fun part of being able to create your own XCOM Soldiers is also super intriguing, basing them off of family, friends, co-workers (hell, twitch subscribers maybe?). And the game has almost endless replayablity, as long as you don't fully commit to the main story, doing  a sort of "Survive as Long as You Can," mode.

While, I really enjoyed the game, there's a few things to be noted. All recruits start with identical base stats, unlike the first game, and the first game that had the variety of "differing," options, is lost here. Though, it's a nice touch that you can throw you're custom characters into a character pool to encounter on a fresh run.

Probably one of the most fun I've had for 6$ on Steam.. And to think, I found this game from a YouTube video lightly mentioning it. Hey, worth it, brochacho.

213284268.review:
After nearly 160 hours on Steam, here’s my take:
TL;DR: Only play this if you’re ready to commit. Even on “Rookie” difficulty, the second half of the game is far from easy. Losing soldiers to wounds or covert missions hurts, so anything less than “flawless” often isn’t an option.

Graphics – 7/10
Solid visuals, though cutscenes feel dated compared to in-game graphics.
Replayability – 7/10
Decent if you leave a few months between runs. Core missions and tech progression make later stages feel repetitive.
Difficulty – 2/10
Even on the lowest setting, expect heavy enemy spawns and challenging missions. Lost missions, in particular, can feel tedious.
Story – 3/10
Well-presented but nothing groundbreaking.
Customisation – 9/10
Outstanding variety for soldiers, especially given the risk of permanent death.
Missions – 5/10
Around 10 mission types with variations: VIP escorts, rescues, sabotage, and timed objectives. DLC missions add flavour.
Sound/Music – 7/10
Catchy soundtrack with memorable themes. Voice options are impressive, though accented English is missing.
Gameplay
Class diversity shines—Sharpshooters and Rangers feel distinct, and late-game gear adds strategic depth. However, Hacker/Medic and Templar/Psionic classes feel underwhelming.

Overall
Worth picking up on discount if you enjoy turn-based tactics. Even in 2025, it holds up well compared to similar titles.

213207310.review:
>Devs didn't know how to handle statistics. This game is only salvageable on easy difficulty. You'll get steamrolled >75% of missions if you select difficulty with common sense. Which means, you are either moderately big brain, or you'll lose even on easy difficulty. Sometimes even with moderately big brain you lose anyway because unregulated RNG is a gaming sin. Prepare to savescum, either a little bit, or a lot, and I suggest from mission select, not combat checkpoints, because the rng feels unchanged when you do that.
>Unpolished. Many expectations of mechanics (like clicking on a mech to know if if it has overpowered grenades that are imminently about to kill half your squad) are met with nothing (clicking on a robot does nothing, come on, even Fire Emblem tells you your enemy information, doesn't make it any less of a strategy game).
>Tough to learn initially. Many basic lessons the game should teach you but doesn't will lead to overwhelming loss.
>>Lesson to move half a turn at a time? Dead squad.
>>Lesson to identify the enemy as early in your turns as possible so you have turns to reduce their firepower and manpower? Another dead squad.
>>Lesson that snipers can't automatically fire at long distances without a special skill even though you can tell "automatically shoot" to your long vision sniper character? Another dead squad.
>>Vehicles can suddenly explode unrealistically? Another dead squad. 
>Has many glitches, only salvageable with bugfix mod collections. Be aware that the tutorial is very very strict, I found a menu softlock that only happened because I didn't equip the medikit it wanted me to equip, you equip it, and the softlock goes away. When in doubt, do what the game tells you. And listen carefully to what it tells you, because you can miss it. Also, when you launch the mod launcher and enter the mod selection menu, give it 30 seconds, it lies to you saying you have no mods installed because it has to finish reading every mod to display them.

Can you have fun with this game? Yes. Does it require patience? Yes. Does it require mods? Yes. Does it require a significant amount of brainpower? Yes. Is it overall very flawed? Yes. Should you get it? Only on sale. What about DLC? I haven't gotten it, but all of it costs triple the price of the base game, and I genuinely don't think it adds triple the value.

"XCOM 2" is a brilliant evolution of the turn-based tactical strategy genre, significantly raising the stakes by placing the player in command of a global resistance fighting against an alien-controlled Earth.

the game truly excels in its deeply satisfying, high-risk, high-reward tactical combat, which is brilliantly complemented by the compelling strategic layer of managing the Avenger mobile base and the global rebellion. With procedurally generated maps, a massive variety of challenging enemy types, and an array of rewarding soldier customization and class specialization options, XCOM 2 offers unrivaled replayability. Its core design forces players into constant adaptation and tough, permanent choices, making every successful mission feel like a hard-fought and glorious victory.

212710264.review: I haven't actually finished this game yet due to the frustrating RNG, but it was still pretty fun overall. Graphics are solid, and the overhead navigation is intuitive. Gameplay is hard, but gained skill and knowledge of enemies helps. Still, the terrain is randomly generated, so sometimes (especially on timed missions) you just have to tank turns and lose a guy or two. With how level-up progression works, it's tough to come back from permanently losing a top squad member, because it puts you at a severe disadvantage in the tough final levels. At least you can save at each turn if you want to run something back or avoid a bad RNG play, but that can be very time consuming. Wouldn't recommend getting until it's on sale for ~$20, and DLC is probably a pass. Try the base game, and consider DLC after.

You will mourn when your colonel dies after 30 succesfull mission and 50+ alien killes, because of the stupidity of a rookie or the simple luck of an alien shot. No one can replace him. And you realize that this is the tipping point of the campaign and you will loose the war after the coming series of failed missions, because you loose the veterans able to withstain the strains of the battlefield. Although your newbies will have the best equipment, they never will have the experience needed to win the war.  Still you fight on for a loosing cause, because there is no alternative. 209753841.review:
XCOM 2 — игра, к которой я возвращаюсь снова и снова уже много лет. При том что у меня большая библиотека хороших игр. Очень продуманная пошаговая тактика, одновременно динамичная и адреналиновая: каждая миссия как мини‑боевик, где одно неверное движение может сломать всю кампанию.

Вчера начал новую кампанию на высокой сложности в режиме терминатора. Дошёл до миссии, где впервые появляется Ассассин на мосту — заранее готовился, что потеряю половину ключевых солдат. Но всё вышло наоборот: из миссии я ушёл всего с одним ранением, которое нанёс обычный огнеметчик, а не босс. Ассассина я засёк сразу после его спавна и с одного хода просто уничтожил. На этой сложности такое вызывает прямое уважение к самому себе.

Тактика в игре глубокая: считай каждый шаг, думай наперёд, строй базу, развивай отряд, выбирай миссии. Ошибся — сам виноват.

Плюсы:
- глубокая и требовательная тактика
- реально разные враги с разными подходами
- миссии не повторяются по ощущениям
- глобальная стратегия важна так же, как и бой

Минусы:
- на высоких сложностях ошибок не прощает
- новичкам придётся потеть
