209684569.review:
Deep inside your flying base is a bar dedicated to fallen soldiers. It has a list of each soldier’s name, time of death, and the operation they died in. It’s a trigger for dark memories. Reading down I remember one soldier crushed to death by a giant snake, another burnt to death in a ruined office. Another shot dead then psychically resurrected to fight her friends. Operation Dismal Window was a bad day for rookie Neel Mehra. Sergeant Flynn Hudson bit the dust in Operation Half-Eaten Tears.

If it wasn’t obvious from XCOM’s ominous naming convention for operations, this is a tough gig. But these are the heroes you’ll remember when the lasers stop. These are the fallen stars in this gruelling, outstanding strategy game.

Humanity seems doomed from the outset in XCOM 2. The game assumes you failed in your attempts to repel the alien invasion in Enemy Unknown. Now Earth’s citizens live a coddled life under the totalitarian control of the aliens and their co-opted soldiery, Advent. The resistance lives on only in the form of a few determined soldiers, scientists and engineers who have managed to repurpose a huge alien ship, the Avenger. This is your home.

A detailed cross-section of the vessel lets you zoom into rooms to initiate research and building projects. A central cluster of rooms can be cleared out to build new facilities, and on the bridge you access the Geoscape, a map of the world that lets you choose where you want to park your spacecraft.

To fight back, you must expand your reach from your lone starting territory by contacting nearby resistance groups. Time is frozen on the Geoscape, but when you park over an objective—make resistance contact; acquire resources; contact the black market—you activate a timer and spend precious days to claim it. This is nerve-racking. At any moment your scans can be interrupted by an alien attack, or a mission that will let you attack the aliens. You can choose to ignore some of these, but it’s not wise. Missions net you important resources, give your soldiers a chance to gain experience, and counter ‘Dark Events'—varied alien initiatives that, among many options, can half your income for a month, or send an interceptor out to hunt The Avenger.

Combat is turn-based, and takes place on procedural battlefields that are uncannily well generated. Only once have I seen a truck spawned partly into a wall. For the vast majority of battles the terrain is busy, interesting, and benefits from a huge leap in visual fidelity from Enemy Unknown. The snowy forests, slums, city centres and alien bases are varied both in decorative assets such as sleek futuristic cars and fluffy trees, and in the vertical variation provided by cliffs and multi-storey buildings.

They blow up nicely, too. I’ll commonly grenade a wall to remove cover and offer my soldiers highchance shots at a target. To my surprise, I also discovered that soldiers running around above ground level can fall through the floor if they’re in a burning building. Explosions can start fires that propagate, blocking floor tiles. One of my grenades started a fire next to a terminal I had to hack. Sending a soldier to stand in that fire and hack the console was not one of my finest XCOM moments.

The four soldier classes have been revamped. Enemy Unknown players will recognise the suppressive heavy weapon capabilities of the grenadier, and the sniping ability of the sharpshooter, but both can access new abilities that alter their jobs. The sharpshooter’s gunslinger skills, earned by levelling up, can turn them into an effective mid-range pistolier who can take out multiple enemies in style, while the grenadier can improve the explosive potential of their grenade launcher, effectively merging Enemy Unknown’s heavy and support roles in one class.

The other two classes are less familiar. The specialist has a drone, which can move around the battlefield to heal allies, zap enemies, or hack alien comms towers, and also has robots to disable or even temporarily steal units. The ranger fills the speedy, close-range role formerly occupied by the assault class, but they have a sword for high-risk, high-damage charge attacks, and therefore win. They can also pursue the scout skill track, which lets them strike very effectively from stealth.

Once concealment is broken, life becomes much more difficult. Successful shots are dictated by chance rolls, and you secure favourable odds by staying in good cover and flanking. A poor move or a stroke of bad luck can wipe out a soldier, or take them out of action for days. Time-limited objectives to hack a terminal or rescue/assassinate a VIP in a certain number of turns force you to be reckless. What’s more, all alien variants, bar the lowest tier of enemy soldier, have the capacity to be incredibly disruptive. The lowly Sectoids of Enemy Unknown are all grown up, and can mind-control your troops and resurrect corpses.

I won’t ruin the surprise and horror of the more advanced alien troops, but a couple left me in despair after a massacre, wondering if I had the soldiers and the ability to go on. I slowly fought back. I recruited new troops, built new technology, got better at the game, and was left elated, feeling I’d conquered a impossible task. Strategy games just don’t normally feel like this.

There’s more you’re better off discovering for yourself, like the weapon mods, extensive troop customisation, Psi-ops warriors, exo-skeletal suits and the story, told over a series of special missions. I can find little to criticise. The camera occasionally wafts through walls in close-ups, there’s sometimes a lengthy pause before the character you’re watching acts. A lack of foreknowledge in your first playthrough will hurt your ability to plan, too, forcing you to be more reactive, though the constant flow of new enemy types and story missions makes up for that. Thanks to your varying starting position, procedural missions and tactical depth, XCOM 2 can and should be played repeatedly.

Verdict: 9/10

XCOM 2 scratches an itch that no other game does when it comes to wardrobe customization, character sculpting, and creating elite squads. The strategy and gameplay are excellent—and often rage-inducing—but this can be managed with the right doctrines and tactics.

Yes, you will miss 90% shots if you take enough of them. That’s how probability works. If you’re OCD about that sort of thing, save-scum to your heart’s content. If you’re an achievement hunter and enjoy a challenge, don’t.

Back to the main point: the game’s customization options are incredible, especially with the right mods. You can have clones fighting stormtroopers, Titanfall pilots dropping in to decimate the alien threat, and if you want to listen to the Demoman while committing war crimes—you absolutely can.

208306747.review:
This game doesn’t want you to win. It wants to watch you suffer. You’ll name your squad after your friends, send them on a “simple” mission, and five minutes later, Samad the sniper misses a 95% shot and gets vaporized by a Sectoid or random Trooper. You’ll rage-quit, swear you’re done… and then boot it up again five minutes later.

It’s the perfect mix of strategy, chaos, and emotional damage. Managing your base feels like juggling grenades, and every mission feels like a movie directed by your anxiety. You’ll scream, you’ll panic, you’ll celebrate, and then you’ll cry when your MVP grenadier gets one-shot.

But when you do win—when that desperate plan somehow works—it’s pure bliss. You feel like the smartest person alive. For about five minutes. Until the next mission.

Played on rookie, played on mods. Enjoyable with and without indeed. 

⭐ 11/10 – Tactical pain, emotional trauma, and the best “one more mission” game ever made.
(Remember: 95% means 5%.)(yes you will miss)

208302478.review:
It's the gaming equivalent of sadism and being addicted to it.

I'm am truly scared of this game and sometimes find a brave day to play it on both on my PC and my phone. It's a lot of fun to watch other pro players play it but I don't think I could ever beat it with the difficulty scaling eventually getting too high. Too many losses can bring down your spirit and make it impossible to beat the game. I'd like to think I've gotten better about it but I still get stuck in the early game.

Adding War of the Chosen raises the difficulty, adds a bigger story and more unit types to the game. The vanilla XCOM 2 resembles XCOM 1 a lot more and is probably a bit easier to play with fewer features and challenges to deal with. Don't expect to win every mission as keeping your soldiers alive is more important.

Speaking of soldiers, the default character customization is pretty good but limited when compared to the sims. Obviously, this is made better thanks to mods but I hope the next game will have a more thorough character customizer that rivals that of a MMO game. Being able to add entire backstories to characters is amazing and I've spent hours writing fan fiction with it. We love and care for our characters and cry when they die.

Speaking of MODS, it's obviously the big reason the game is still super popular to this day. I only use voice mods to add characters, clothes and new faces to the game. There are mods for anything you want that changes the game drastically but I mostly just stick the voice mods.

So, hope to see an XCOM 3 probably never. XCOM 2 will probably live forever.

What's the line in which Random becomes a problem? Xcom, is that line. 

I think this is a unique case of a game in which most loops work, but the primary loop doesn't. In the end of the day, I can take only so many 70% shots missing in a row before this is not worth my time. Chance plays far too big of a role in the mechanics and while surely they will average out in infinity, they won't in a mission nor a playthough. Increasing the dificulty only to have the dice more heavily weighted against you seems like a tough sell. 

If you wish to play a skill game whose results are heavily influenced by chance, play Poker. It's a better time, more dynamic and you can play with friends. And the kicker, its less influenced by chance than this game.

207643649.review:
After 30 years of playing games, I recognized that probably this is the most challenging and well thought-out game in existence as of today. The legendary difficulty is very well balanced, it's like  there was a separate team to work on this difficulty level alone. Additionally I don't remember another game where all components make real sense: there are no useless items, attributes, buildings, mechanics etc. There is no junk content. The only reason I am not sure it is my favorite game is:
1) It has a "timer",  which can be annoying, but in reality it's only a limit to the maximum number of turns: think of it as solving a problem in X steps.  So this can - at least theoretically - improve tactical game-play.
2) High level of randomization. Well certainly this doesn't improve the tactical aspect, but it increases the RP and (slightly) the strategy aspect. It is responsible for creating the story of the player, otherwise it would be boring and too disconnected from reality.
3) The (game) story is mediocre at best. But there is not too much text, so one could just ignore it. And it is not immersion breaking and childish like the Midnight Suns (for older players).

I was a big fan of the original XCOM and I thought I'd like this one too, but there is something that is off about it.

The more I played this game the more I came to see the planning I did in combat didn't really matter.  What I mean by this is when you start your turn you can move you team in any order.  Say you move your team of 6 guys to 6 certain spots and then fire on a mob.  All of your guys miss.  If you reload the game and, [i]in a different order[/i], move all of those guys to those [i]exact same spots[/i] and take a shot... now they all hit.

How is this possible?  The character's accuracy hasn't changed, the position on the map hasn't changed.  Why am I playing all "tactical" when the hits and misses are arbitrarily assigned before I've even taken my turn?

This ended up being a total fun-sucking game mechanic that made me not even want to play the game.

206852041.review:
Played the game ages ago and finished the main game on Xbox. Rebought the game on steam so I could get the expansions but then stopped playing for a while. Came back to find it had a launcher. Which I've seen had caused a lot of issues for people and I've had them too.. But since then the launcher has been removed and things seem to have been fixed. 

I had played the game on and off and originally had more written about bugs with the launcher and so on, but since then the launcher has been removed and so I've removed mention of the bugs. Weather they are still in the original version (without the expansions) I do not know. But now that I've finished it with all expansions I decided to finish off my review.

Xcom is a tactical team turned based game where you are playing as the humans retaking earth from invading aliens who had succeeded in taking over. You start with a squad of 4 per mission but can eventually be upgraded to 6. You gain multiple levels of armour and weapon upgrades as you play through the game to make characters stronger. Character deaths are permanent but easily replaced at the cost of Supply resources. Though in the beginning replacing people can be costly and difficult, replacing too many means spending what little resources you have to rehire new recruits and thus making it difficult to buy much needed upgrades, as well as gain any kind of rank on soldiers. Doing well at the start definitely sets the pace for the rest of the game. Fail at the start and you'll struggle if not fail completely and likely just easier to start over (which i had done my first time playing).

Each character is customisable with looks including different races/skin colours, gender, voices (as well as other languages), hair style/colour and choosing colour and patterns on armour and weapons. Completely optional as it makes no difference what someone looks like.

A variety of enemies though not a large amount. And majority of them are fairly simple, later enemies get more powers that can definitely make things rough for you.

As for storyline, there isn't really much. There's a bunch of little "stories" through it that you play through missions and on the side. Such as the "Chosen" who are 3 Assassins designed to hunt and take you down as well as a few other special enemies that you can unlock who will appear randomly in missions.

Graphics i'd say is good more so the detail for this style of game, but there is also a lot of clipping in the game and extreme lack of physics for a lot of things. It seems the physical placement of objects has absolutely no bearing on weather a shot can happen or not, you'll just shoot through a concrete pillar like its made of butter and watch as the pillar explodes while doing very minor damage to whatever fleshy enemy you shot at.

After all this time I see nothing has been done about controller support either, if anything seems far worse than when I played on PC the first time. Can't even choose that as an option anymore, stuck with keyboard/mouse. Coming from playing the first Xcom and originally playing Xcom 2 on the xbox, I got too use to the controller and I was hoping to continue that.

The concept of Xcom I love. I don't know of any other game that works like Xcom does, but if there is any, I would definitely love to play them. Simply because its a one of kind game (to me) I keep coming back to it just so I can enjoy how it plays. I would definitely love to see this concept and style very much expanded on, with more weapons, enemies, abilities and allies. Playing the expansions has opened up some and it had just made me want more. Weather it be Xcom or some other game.

Now onto Chimera Squad.

I've played other games that try to nail the same formula and they just can't quite get it right - not XCOM right. 10 years later and it's still the top of the genre.

XCOM 2 basically just expands XCOM and keeps going, while fixing a few of the little annoyances. It's Final Fantasy Tactics + Fallout Tactics but with an alien invasion setting, and difficulty setting that scales to inhuman levels for lots of replayability. If you've never tried it and you like turn-based strategy games, it's 100% worth buying.

206167784.review:
This was my first game in the genre of "send small squads of your OCs to die in horrific circumstances" and and as a certified OC torturer, I'm quite satisfied.

I would recommend playing the game once without War of the Chosen enabled, then doing a playthrough with it. The base game is good on its own, but WotC overhauls enough systems that you'll appreciate it more if you play without it first. Do note that most mods are designed to work with either the base game or WotC, not both -- keep an eye on that in the Steam workshop. If you are downloading a lot of mods or are unsure which mods are for which version of the game, consider using AML2 ("Alternative Mod Launcher") which you can find on GitHub. That being said, the in-game mod manager is good enough for most people.

This game previously suffered from Crappy Publisher-Specific Launcher Syndrome, but the 2K launcher has since been removed.

I love this game ever since I played XCOM enemy unknown. Everyone that complains about overwhelming odds for either XCOM enemy unknown or XCOM 2 doesn't know how to strategize or plan out their engagements. 9 times out of 10 when I launch a squad I never lose a single member in the squad, Im tactical at first with 4 people but by the time I get to 6 for a squad its all out war and I just have to test myself how far I can get everyone in position before I pull the plug on the enemy.

make no mistake this game can be difficult but if you play a few times and work out the mechanics you don't have to be careful as much as you want to be aggressive. when you start out, play it safe and slow. when you get several people around the rank of captain that is when the enemy better start running and hiding. anyone who also complains about the rng in this game doesn't realize that you get no time limits in most missions and if you want to . . . boot up a save file from before a mess up (there is no shame in admitting that you make a mistake) and correct yourself. the enemy only makes a decision based on the action you do, so if you make a mistake twice in the same stage with the same character, the ai is going to respond in the same manner every single time.

I played on console before pc and Ive played several games and lost a total of 9 squad members for XCOM 2 and around 12 for enemy unknown. I barely played enemy within but it basically is what XCOM 2 was before it came out. so I would definitely say this game is worth the price tag that you see for it, whether its on sale or not.

205099311.review:
Hands down the greatest turn based tactical game on the market, even all these years later (which isn't a surprise considering the franchise's roots).

Bringing together the perfect mix of tactical gameplay, soldier customisation and levelling, strategic layer decision-making, and simple but meaningful base-building, the game is honestly just timeless (optimisation aside).

The RNG is absolutely brutal at times, but is a core staple of the series - commander difficulty is great, but legend is just.... well that's somethin' else entirely. No matter how many hours you've put in, you'll regularly find yourself at the edge of your seat with your fingers crossed waiting on the outcome of a shot - sometimes screaming in joy when it actually lands and saves your whole mission, but just as often launching your keyboard across the room after missing a 99% hit at point blank range.

It's one of those games you'll find yourself coming back to every few years for a new run, as whilst there are (by now) many alternatives in a similar style, nothing hits quite like the OG. The game constantly rewards you for forward-thinking moves and strategies, as well as equally punishes you for rash decisions and missteps. It's very addictive.

Having finished the game twice way back on Ps4, and now having sunk another near-300 hours on PC, the proof is in the pudding, really.

That's not even touching upon the hours and hours of additional content, cosmetics, enemies, gameplay systems, and overhauls, found on the workshop (Covert Infiltration especially, which completely expands the strategic layer of the game).

All in all, I freakin' love this game, and will probably never stop coming back.

205059882.review:
5/10 Recommended on Sale
Its a good game but somewhat incomplete which the DLCs attempted to fix but with additional unbalancing and higher and harder and timed difficulty curves.
In terms of the game play and graphics it is vastly improved over xcom1 with the awesome action and some more interesting 3D shots. But it goes back a bit with other stuff like unit types (which the DLC boosted a bit but not far enough) and the stealth action was grown but not enough in my opinion and they really should have made it more complex. As once your seen suddenly everyone will spot you straight away which didn't make sense... But still better then xcom 1 which didn't have much at all.
Other features grew from features in xcom1 though they removed the fighter planes and strike action which were quite fun. And many of the building features aern't as complex as they used to be. 
The DLCs added alot and in some parts felt like they added features that should have really been in the main game. Though some features they added just made some missions seem ridiculous. The random chosen appearing in missions and alien rulers were a nightmare and would mess with the focus of game. They would always be overpowered and ridiculous. Especially in terms of the rulers who (if played without the combined dlc mode) would be ridiculous and overpowered and react on player moves which was a ridiculous feature in a turn based game.
In terms of war of the chosen The extra allies they added were useful and at first interesting though.... they really lacked some depth. They introduce the ring facility which adds covert missions... but unlike xcom1 there aern't missions attached to these so it fealt pointless after a while. Useful for one off items and abilities but never those covert rescue missions which were always pretty fun.
And the allies don't really have many missions attached to them after the intial ones except for the kill the chosen missions which were more annoying then fun really. And never related to the ally who released the mission except that apparent the chosen in question had a weakness to them?!?!
The main story line was ok/good. It needed more depth but was quite interesting. The invasion feature of different continants was removed for a general avatar timer (which could be increased at the beginning) which made the game somewhat easier though i never liked the invasion feature in xcom one were you could suddenly lose continents. But overall the story was ok
Overall the game is good/ok but had loads of holes in it. I actually played the game twice but only ever completed it once as it felt like a grind as the game went on. The difficulty ramped up but the story petered out. I felt like xcom 1 atleast kept you going until the end.
I recommend this game if on sale only!

205048240.review:
It’s a game about overcoming overwhelming odds, meaning it won’t be for everyone.

Personally, the game really clicked with me. Not once did I feel like battles were impossible, every defeat felt like the result of a miscalculation on my part, or of leaving too much up to chance.

In the end, what truly elevates the experience is fighting alongside a squad that feels like yours. When their names carry weight, every victory feels hard-won, and every loss cuts just a little deeper.

If you enjoy turn-based tactics, the threat of permadeath, and the need to plan ahead, XCOM is well worth your time.

Fantastic game well worth full-price. Even more so at the frequent bargain bin sales-rates.
Would recommend buying the base game & at the very least War of the Chosen for a first time player.
The game really becomes whole with the expansion.
The base game is good enough alright, but to stagger out the experience by first playing a "normal"-campaign and then a "War of the Chosen"-campaign, is to your personal detriment.

If unsure; play for a couple of hours. If the game sinks its teeth into you, like it has for me, grab War of the Chosen and start over. Not to worry - plenty of mix-ups in even the first hour.

War of the Chosen is also necessary for modern modding, which is the foundation of a dual-experience game. Which XCOM 2 most definitely is.
E.G. Civilization, Europa Universalis or other Paradox-games, Minecraft, Factorio, Valheim.... etc.. etc...

That is; the developers campaign and systems is worth the price of admission on its own. But the title then gains a second life. 
Not as a curated experience, but your experience; shaped to your particular tastes and interests.

The modding community is simply outstanding! Hats off, all modders.
I'll gladly buy y'all swathes of coffee for my remaining days.

203498187.review:
Edit: the expansion "War of the Chosen" seems to fix all bugs I've mentioned in this review. 

*Hours listed are inaccurate as I mostly play offline. Around 60 hours at time of review*

Game is top to bottom fantastic, and I haven't even tried the dlc or expansion. However do not buy at full price. It's often on sale for $5, wait for that. 

This is due to the many bugs that I've encountered. Here are some annoying ones: 

-Buttons overlapping becoming unclickable
-Buttons that are supposed to do one thing but do another.("make utility items available" exits you out of the pre mission view) 
-Button not clickable at all 
-Various visual things like enemies teleporting. 

All of these things are annoying but are easy to work around and not a problem. 

However, there is one bug that is potentially game ruining. During a post mission screen the game can freeze on a camera shot that is clipped outside your ship. If this happens to you, you have to restart the game. When you restart, there's a good chance that if you load the most recent save file, you'll just continuously hit this bug every time you get to the same screen. I have avoided this by loading a earlier save file, which means you might have to play sections of the mission over again. Again, very annoying but workable. 

Where this starts to become a real problem is with Ironman mode, a difficulty mode that does not allow you to have multiple saves, and forces you to stick with whatever decision you make or whatever happens in your mission. If you hit this bug in Ironman mode you have no other save file to go back to, which bricks your campaign. This has happened to me once and I had to end the campaign (that was going pretty well) about halfway through. 

I'm still recommending the game, as there will be some people that won't come across this issue, but that is not my experience. There are other workarounds that people have found for this bug, but none of them have worked for me. I would fully recommend this game with no hesitation if it wasn't for this bug, which makes Ironman mode more or less unplayable. Game is almost 10 years old, I highly doubt anyone is going to fix this.

203430575.review:
Xcom2—异世界敌后武工队模拟器？
Xcom2—An otherworldly Behind-Enemy-Lines Working Team Simulator?
抗日敌后武工队和回合制战术策略游戏结合起来有多上头？我一直没想明白，为什么没有中国制作组开发抗日背景的回合制战术策略游戏，幸好有xcom2，让我得以体验一把。
How engaging would it be to combine the Chinese behind-enemy-lines working teams from the War of Resistance against Japan with a turn-based tactical strategy game? I’ve never quite understood why no chinese development team has created a turn-based tactical strategy game set in the War of Resistance against Japan. Fortunately, we have XCOM 2, which allows me to experience exactly that.
这游戏里的元素可以完美代入抗日背景：
The elements in this game can perfectly mirror the War of Resistance setting:
装备有代差优势、数量庞大且穷凶极恶的外星人势力—日寇；
The technologically superior, numerous, and ruthless alien forces—the Japanese invaders;
最多6人的小规模战术小队—英勇的敌后武工队；
A small tactical squad of up to six members—the heroic behind-enemy-lines working team;
开局世界已沦陷，xcom只能在敌人占领区打游击—这和当年敌后武工队面临的情况一模一样；
The world is already overrun at the start, and XCOM can only fight guerrillas in enemy-occupied areas—exactly the situation faced by the behind-enemy-lines working teams back then;
可移动的飞船作为基地—敌后武工队神出鬼没的游击打法；
A mobile ship as a base—reflecting the elusive guerrilla tactics of the working teams;
通过解剖敌人获得科技、通过逆向工程仿造武器、攀科技树—没有枪，没有炮，敌人为我造；
Advancing technology by autopsying enemies, reverse-engineering weapons, and climbing the tech tree—“with no rifles, no artillery, the enemy makes them for us”;
和其他抵抗势力合作，收留叛逃过来的变种人、科研人员—抗日民族统一战线；
Cooperating with other resistance forces and sheltering defecting mutants and scientists—the united front in the War of Resistance;
故意不做主线，招兵买马、鸟枪换炮的Longwar打法—持久战。
Intentionally avoiding the main story, recruiting troops, upgrading equipment—the Longwar approach embodies a protracted war.
暂时只想到这些，总而言之，把这个游戏当成异世界敌后武工队模拟器来玩毫无问题。希望有朝一日能玩上国人制作、原汁原味的敌后武工队游戏。
These are just the connections I’ve thought of for now. In short, treating this game as an otherworldly behind-enemy-lines working team simulator works perfectly. I hope someday we can play a genuinely Chinese-made game about the behind-enemy-lines working teams.

203051745.review:
A BELOVED (10 year old) AAA GAME (still very much alive on the Steam Workshop)

Designed by Jake Solomon, XCOM 2 is a masterclass in turn-based strategy and tension. This game is a true rollercoaster ride. You start out weak and outgunned, then a massive surge of new armour and weapons makes you feel unstoppable… until the aliens upgrade again. Cue more desperate research and tactical play until, finally, you can breathe again!

Why it stands out:
Character Customisation (one of my favourite parts of the game). Designing soldiers, naming them, and giving them unique looks makes victories sweeter and losses genuinely devastating. Visiting the Steam Workshop is a must.
Relentless Challenge. The aliens constantly evolve, forcing fresh strategies and keeping the tension high throughout.
Excellent Graphics & Atmosphere. With strong visuals and a gritty, desperate tone.

War of the Chosen DLC is a must-buy, adding even more depth. Be warned, the difficulty spike is intense and not for casual players. This is one of the greatest turn-based strategy games of all time and a true modern classic.

202976144.review:
[Gameplay] 🕹️

The gameplay of XCOM 2 is split between managing your global resistance from the Avenger and commanding a squad in tense, turn-based missions. Every decision on the battlefield is very important, from soldier positioning to using the environment, and shooting the enemy.

[Graphics] 🖼️

The game's visuals capture a high-tech dystopian Earth. Detailed character models for the game's year and varied environments bring the desperate struggle to life, with impressive special effects that add to the cinematic feel of every fight.

[Audio] 👂

I liked the audio in general. The sound effects are distinct, giving the feeling of every battle. The soundtrack builds tension and provides a sense of your resistance efforts.

[Storytelling] ✒️

The narrative is: humanity has lost and is now ruled by the alien-backed ADVENT administration. As a rescued commander, you lead a global resistance from the shadows, fighting to expose their true plans. It's a compelling story of hope and rebellion.

[Difficulty] 💪

This is however a difficult game. I had constant pressure of the "Avatar Project" timer and the game's reliance on chance can be frustrating, but this challenge makes every small victory feel incredibly rewarding. Some missions have turn count, which makes it even harder.

202812246.review:
[h1]The best is the enemy of the good[/h1]
I gave the game plenty of time to win me over. My first playthrough was back in 2018 — around 70 hours on normal difficulty with mods that removed the timers.  [strike]I don’t remember exactly how far I got, but I do remember getting bored and never finishing it.[/strike] Judging by the achievements I actually did finish the final mission in vanila, but I don't remember playing most of the game. I guess this is a case of the brain suppressing traumatic memories.

Now I’ve decided to give it another shot, and oh boy, this game is dragging on. I’ve put in 20 hours on my second run (this time with only quality-of-life mods and on easy difficulty so I could finish it faster), and I’m already burned out. I just unlocked tier 2 armor, and it feels like I’ve barely made any progress.

I enjoy the tactical combat part of the game, but everything else is overwhelming. Constant notifications about the Avatar project progress, to combat that, I need to make intel to unlock regions so I can attack alien bases. Additionally, I need to hunt the Chosens before they become too strong and prevent them from harassing me during missions and attacking the Avenger. For that, I need to level up soldiers to send them on resistance missions so I can reach their strongholds. And to make contact with resistance cells, I have to spend more time clearing out rooms on the Avenger to build a communications center. Finding engineers to expedite the process is also preferable. Meanwhile, I still need to research weapons and armor to keep up with the enemy’s power creep, build other rooms, and progress the main tasks. And if that’s not enough, the game throws extra missions at me nonstop.

The final straw for me was the Alien Hunters DLC bosses. The turn-interrupt mechanic is just a terrible idea. Even though I disabled the DLC, they still showed up. At that point, I had already put in about 10 hours and didn’t want to start over, so I had to cheese the AI and abuse saves-load to beat the Viper King. But when I ran into the Berserker Queen around the 20-hour mark, I was completely burned out. I just didn’t have it in me to deal with that nonsense anymore.

The game just doesn't give me any breathing room. Even when I’m just customizing soldiers, some NPC is always talking in my ear. It’s just too much — constant juggling of tasks with no pause is exhausting. And it goes on and on and on. Playing this game puts me in a constant state of stress. It’s just not for me. I finished the first XCOM twice, each run taking about 20 hours, and I loved it. It’s one of my favorite games. And no, I haven’t tried the Long War mod — and I don’t plan to.

202067135.review:
TLDR: Not just a terrible game, but an “I can’t believe I paid money for this” terrible game. What a waste of my finite life.

[b][u]General Thoughts/Overview:[/u][/b]
Base game review only.

XCOM2 is terrible. No, terrible isn’t enough. XCOM2 is [i]grotesquely dire[/i]. It is low quality, lazily made, poorly executed, and downright detestable [i][b]slop[/b][/i]. This back alley abortion of a game isn’t just a money grab, it’s also unflinchingly after your data. I’d call it an insult, but it isn’t even nuanced or bright enough to manage “yeah, well your mum’s fat!” in the grating high pitched squeal of preschool children.

Before we dive in I want to point out that the game tries to demand [b]ALL[/b] of your information, every time you play. It demands you not only let it look at everything on your computer, but also send that information “Overseas to countries who may not have the same privacy and data protection laws.” I’m good, thanks. 

Right. So, we’re the commander of XCOM, and we failed to stop the alien invasion. For 20 years we’ve been in the hands of the enemy while our guys are doing crosswords and twiddling their thumbs, because apparently we’re the only guy who thinks doing revolutionary things like autopsies or upgrading equipment is a useful/good idea. The aliens have also managed to not find the XCOM base, despite it being on a derelict alien ship they’re looking for, AND them having enough time and technology to easily locate it. But now we’re back, and its time to take the battle to them! Just in the nick of time and all because the xenos are up to something, and it probably isn’t good for us.

As a man who does firmly believe in autopsies, let’s crack open this one and find out exactly how much cancer it had. 

[b][u]Pros:[/u][/b]
[list][*] Story premise. The basic idea behind the continuation of the game is actually really novel and I like it a lot. If they could have made the remnants of XCOM a believable thing, maybe lent into the network of disconnected and disorganised resistance, set it similarly to xcom:apocalypse but as a resistance movement… The raw idea was good.
[*] I quite liked the integration of the alien corpses into squad tech and upgrades. I can’t remember if it was in the first remake or not, but it felt right to have it here, turning the agents of the enemy into the weapons we use against them. 
[*] The boardgameification of the main story in the overworld was an interesting touch. I liked the way it made the world feel dynamic, with the ebb and flow of the war, and the feeling of the aliens progress weighing down along with the almost inevitability of their victory, if we don’t get them first. 
[/list]

[b][u]Cons:[/u][/b]
I could write far more words than this review page would allow detailing the many and catastrophic failures of this ‘game’. Instead I’ll limit myself to the three critical pillars of a game, and detail how this doesn’t match any of them.

[list][*] Story failure: The good premise is shot down by an abysmal story full of plot holes, contrivances, and irrational choices made out of character by supposedly intelligent and rational people. Even putting aside the mostly predictable ‘twists’ the game fails at almost every level to inspire any real emotion beyond a general level of “oh. I see.”
[*] Core mechanics failure: The biggest failing of the game, I suppose, is that it isn’t fun. The battle mechanics have been simplified to the point of tedium. The poor explanation of which skills do what, which ones end your turn or not, which abilities in your binary pair did you pick and aren’t bugged, how well did RNGesus bless you today, and the oversimplification of [i]everything[/i] along the way. The base building mechanics are shallow, with research and engineering just horribly neglected and either so impactful that it makes the game fundamentally easier with every step, or so unimpactful as to have been a complete waste of time. The staffing and the absence of a function or use for most members and no choices to be made over them really, really sucks. Even the requisition and mission choice is just badly done. The core mechanics feel like someone wrote down a bullet-point list of things to flesh out, and then they got fired and the ideas were taken as complete. 
[*] Artistic failure: I really don’t like judging the artwork of a game, but this can’t go unchallenged. Every single aspect of the art in this is a failure. The music and ambient sounds are poorly done, they fail to evoke any emotion at all and are fortunate to just fade away. The graphics are basic at best, but the issue is the clipping. The assets are so badly put together and the game so poorly designed that it just becomes comical. It was when my sniper spun in slow motion, put his arms and rifle inside the rock he was stood behind, and proceeded to fire a round through the rock, through the back of the guy in front’s head, and trace the traveling blast with excruciating plodding monotony along a million miles of air, through a wall, more air, and then [i]missed the enemy he couldn’t even see anyway[/i] forcing me to endure the tortured screams of the slowed down sounds for a full 5-10 seconds that I really lost any semblance of rosy retrospection and hope that it’d get back to its roots and recover. The voice acting is also terrible. We have all these different cultures and countries supposedly coming together, with names which are generated and come out weird and inaccurate, [i]but then no effort to use this.[/i] If you’re going to put flags on peoples armour, at least give them accurate names! But if you’re going to do that then you could at least give them a single line in their own languages. But nope, everyone speaks in a flat boring American accent. This absence of effort is directly and comically paired with “THE SPOKESMAN” who fails my new and one-day patented “Darishnoo’s Takeaway Test”. The test is simple, can you imagine the person successfully ordering a takeaway in character? If yes, they’re acted well enough to have a level of verisimilitude, if not, or if the poor sap at the other end would think it’s a prank, it’s badly done.  
[/list]

[b][u]Suggested improvements:[/u][/b]
[list][*] Flesh out the base building and management portion of the game.
[*] Make the mission choices and consequences meaningful. 
[*] Fix combat, fix the bugs, just fix everything.
[/list]


[b][u]Overall Recommendation:[/u][/b]
In 1994 I played UFO: Enemy Unknown on a mates PC. In 1995 I bought it in the January sales with my birthday + Christmas money. I’ve played a round of it almost every year since. I was alright with the first reboot, it was different. It wasn’t UFO, but it was ok. A different game for a different time, it wasn’t the suspense filled grinding war of attrition and panic built to be half horror half scramble… and that’s ok. 

This though? This is just a diseased wretch. It’s a parasite defiling the good name of a great game. It lacks the complexity, the nuance, the depth of the first game, a game built where every single line of code mattered and you didn’t get many of them, that somehow was bigger, more bombastic, more sprawling, more complex, and with so much more character. It lacks the first iteration excuses and different style of the remake, they made this once already, and arguably did a better job of it. XCOM2 didn’t fix the problems of XCOM1, it didn’t grow the world, it didn’t build on anything, it didn’t create or add or grow, it’s just here to take your money, your data, and several hours of your life. 

This isn’t ‘for’ anyone, except maybe the shareholder. 

Avoid at all costs, I wasted precious hours of my life to warn you.

201946514.review:
7/10 tentative thumbs up

To put this review into perspective this is my first time jumping back into the series after xcom: ufo defense(ufo: enemy unknown).

It's a good grid based roguelike, but if you really want to feel like you're masterminding the defense of earth from an alien invasion I highly recommend Xenonauts instead. I don't appreciate the design philosophy AAA gaming studios bring to these titles, xcom ufo defense was incredibly ahead of its time for being a grid based strategy set within an alien invasion defense simulator, i feel like if we went back in time and showed 1994 xcom: ufo defense players what the series looks like after 22 years they would be incredibly confused at how little the game has moved forward in any ways except for graphics and accessibility and be even more confused at how much the game has regressed in 22 years.

Xcom 2 really feels like a mission based alien invasion themed grid based strategy game, is it bad at what its trying to do? Not at all, it is a very well designed grid based strategy with good graphics and engaging choices to make. BUT is it a good alien invasion simulator? God no, the gameplay loop isn't even comparable to ufo defense or xenonauts. From a design point of view Xcom 2 feels to me like another page in the eternal dumbing down of modern gaming, modern gamers couldn't be trusted to choose where to apply their defenses or have to make serious decisions about how to allocate resources, instead they are given a choose your own adventure style of decision making in what is basically a completely linear campaign.

Is xcom 2 a bad game? No, it is just a much different game than its predecessors designed for a modern audience. Xcom 2 features more impactful unit decisions with its class system and takes away the trial and error of basebuilding and logistics. I just feel like we've went from a 4x title that truly made you feel like you were fighting off an alien invasion to a fun alien themed grid based strategy with roguelike elements. 

If i had to invest the time into playing a game like this, I'd rather be playing a modern final fantasy tactics.

If i had to invest the time into playing the game this should of been, I'd rather be playing Xenonauts.

Masochists, sycophants, ... maybe femboys, I'm not sure what they even are, but get ready.  This game is a statistical abortion were you get to keep the unwanted baby and give birth all over again. 201134241.review:
I'll start this by saying I LOVE difficult games, and I love strategy games. I've been wanting to try XCOM for years, and this most recent sale I snagged it. I have never genuinely despised and been so off put in the first five hours of a promising game. 

This game isn't hard. it's not even particularly difficult. It's ANNOYING. What isn't explained to you is that no matter how well you play, you're not intrinsically meant to win scenarios. They are beyond brutal, and in the most frustrating ways. Say you're playing this completely blind, and don't want to save scum. This game will randomly add in new elements of gameplay that will completely wipe HOURS of a mission in a split second, and there's absolutely nothing you could have done to stop it unless you had already failed it before or went in with spoilers. I can see how that could be perceived as fun? To a psycopath I guess

The menus aren't designed well. It's incredibly easy to tap a key if you're a new player and completely lose your turn with no explanation as to why. That was really fun to figure out. "The game would have told you that if you'd kept playing". No, the game intended for you to run in and fail so I could learn that's a possibility. It straight up tells you that. EXCEPT YOUR CHARACTERS ARE PERMADEAD FOR THE "INSTRUCTION" EXPERIENCE. I understand that's not a flaw of the game, it's a design element. That doesn't mean it's not bad. 

It'd be one thing if it was just oh you might lose a character or two early on making simple mistakes. No. It's constant and in areas that don't even make sense. For example, I cleared an entire mission. Ran around for 15 minutes because the round wouldn't end and there were no enemies left. I already blew up the enemy beacon? Why isn't it over? No dialogue, no prompt, no subtitle, no warning. Finally, I walk back into the room I blew up the beacon in the first place. BOOM 3 mobs are now in my face and I lose my best person. I'm not okay with that? Why the ♥♥♥♥ would I be okay with losing a character to a bug (THAT HAPPENED 3 TIMES)

It happened in at least 30% of missions. Out of nowhere the fog of war will vanish and an entire swarm will have been roaming around you. The mobs are cranked to the absolute max in these scenarios too, so even playing safe it's not uncommon for five to just run up on you with two bonus turns and you're just dead. "It's level design". Sure, ♥♥♥♥♥♥ level design. 

The solution? Save scum constantly throughout a level or face blatantly unfun surprises that will just wipe your party with no warning or counterplay. You literally cannot do anything in these scenarios unless you just happened to pick that gear for that specific mission, which I should let you know, the game doesn't always tell you exactly what you'll be doing. If you're like me and were hoping for this to be a real tactical strategy, just go in knowing it's close, but it's not balanced. I'd accept the "Get good" comments, if that were a possibility. Failing because of lack of information that wasn't provided isn't fun.

Plus enemies have access to abilities and better overwatch than what we have in the beginning. the game will say a mission is "easy" but that is a lie because it will have 10 enemies and we have a squad of 4 and can easily get overrun or time out in a 5 turn mission. I hate this game. It was cool at first. Love blinds. It was new, it was cool, i loved the 1st one, but coming back to it this game is just terrible! I prefer Divinity 1/2 and Balders Gate 3. 199979821.review:
A classic in turn-based strategy games which offers a variety of ways to play, set difficulties, and player freedom to tailor your own experience. I have put countless hours in both on Steam and console over the course of years, and have yet to get bored. If you played a previous XCOM title and didn't enjoy it, this one is worth a try. If you've already played XCOM 2 for hundreds of hours, give a previous title a try.

XCOM 2, however, is uniquely satisfying and stands out brightly in the stagnation of modern turn-based titles. It even holds up years after release. Every game of its kind will forever be judged by this title, at least for the foreseeable future.

If you're hesitant, the game is often on sale by Steam, but honestly sixty dollars is a fair price to this day. You should wait for  a bundle and grab the DLC though, too!

199911111.review: I really enjoy the unit customization in this game, both in ability trees and appearance. This makes every unit feel unique and raises the steaks for losing them in combat. The abilities for each individual unit are diverse enough to allow for multiple build paths for the same type of unit. WOTC DLC expands this even further, with the ability to cross train some unit abilities, making the best of both worlds for certain unit types. The game play is fun and challenging, and while there is a steep learning curve for those brand new to these type of games, there is a lot of fun and entertainment once you zero it in. You can also save scum your way through the learning curve and play fair once you know what all the enemy types can do, what the rules are, etc. There's really a lot of ways to play and a lot of fun to be had here. I highly recommend this game if you're looking for something to sink your teeth into and a challenge to master.

199812265.review:
Addictive, Tactical, and Personal—A Turn-Based Classic

One of my most played games ever. I’m a huge fan of turn-based strategy, and this one absolutely delivers. Customizing units and growing attached to them adds a personal touch. I find myself crafting little stories during missions and doing everything I can to keep my squad alive.

That said, squad sizes in your barracks can get a bit excessive. I usually stick to the same core rotation, and managing too many units sometimes feels unnecessary or overwhelming. The campaign also runs a bit long—it could use some trimming to keep the pacing tight.

Combat is tense and satisfying, though the RNG can be frustrating—missing multiple 70%+ shots in a row feels unfair, even if it’s technically realistic. Some items and gear also feel redundant, quickly outclassed by later unlocks.

Still, the gameplay, visuals, sound design, and variety of enemies (especially the DLC ones) make for an awesome experience. The alien forces are intimidating, and I love the challenge they bring—though some of the DLC hunters can feel a little over-tuned.

Despite a few pacing and balance issues, this is a top-tier strategy title. If you enjoy tactical depth and squad-based combat, it’s a must-play.

199074123.review:
I thoroughly enjoyed this game, it's everything you want it to be and you don't feel shorted playing it.  It's punishing when you make mistakes and you shouldn't take it's gameplay lightly - your brain needs to be on and consider all possible weakpoints in your strategy. The IP is just awesome.

What's fun about this game is that outside of missions there's a little management game going on.  You must decide if you want to pursue certain resources (including getting more engineers/soldiers/scientists), what missions to take and not take - and they all have opportunity cost such as certain reinforcements, and what facilitiles to build at your base that make certain things easier.  

It truly does not hold your hand and makes things tough, and I really like that! It's up there with my favorite strategy games of all time.  It scratched an itch I didn't realize I had until I played it, now onto Chimera Squad!

P.S. You should play with the WOTC expansion pack, it helps things to be more cinematic IMO.

198783048.review:
I've poured hundreds of hours into this thing across multiple platforms, and it's easily one of my favorite games of all time. Why? Because it's a brutal, unfair, brilliant tactical masterpiece that actively enjoys kicking you in the teeth... and somehow, you keep coming back for more.

The core loop is simple: humanity is oppressed, and you, Commander, are the last hope. You build up your ragtag resistance, research alien tech, and send your highly trained (and usually doomed) soldiers on desperate missions. The tactical combat is just incredible , every move matters, every decision can lead to glorious victory or spectacular failure. And that's where the stories happen.

Like "Private Jenkins." Oh, Jenkins. This was early in a campaign, and I needed him to flank a Muton. Easy shot, 95% chance to hit. Piece of cake. So, I move him. He misses the shot, and then gets critically hit by a Sectoid who then mind-controls him. Jenkins, my rookie, turns around and blows up the evac point with a grenade killing some of my best.  Jenkins eventually got put down by one of his former squadmates. Good times. That's XCOM, baby.

What makes this game truly shine, beyond the core brilliance, is the modding community. Seriously, if you're playing XCOM 2 without mods, you're missing like half the game. From quality-of-life improvements that make sense to absolutely insane custom classes, new enemies, and hilariously broken weapons, mods just elevate this game to another dimension. They fix things, they break things in fun ways, and they keep the replayability infinite.

XCOM 2 is a strategic addiction. It's frustrating, it's hilarious, it's incredibly deep, and it constantly generates unique, unforgettable narratives, mostly about how badly you screwed up. If you like turn-based tactics, high stakes, and watching your elite squad get wiped by a stray alien fart, you need to play XCOM 2. And then mod the hell out of it.

198758989.review:
It's one of the best strategy games by turns but if you don't like this kind of game don't even try it.

The story is old school hardcore sci-fi with a lot of aliens, invasion of Eath and green stuff. All the classics. Particularly, it's the progression of the narrative that although simplist, it is captivating. The big attraction - it is like watching one of those old movies... you are defending the planet and against the odds, maggot! Who wants to live forever?!

And the game is hard. You will die a lot. Especially if you decide to go after some of the most popular mods... and I recommend them. Online game is dead, but who needs them?! Xcom 2 is for you and your willingness to kill aliens in as many ways as you can with a lot of cannon fodder or saving scum all the way.

This is the game that made a lot of companies give up producing strategy turn war games simply because after all these years it still is played by a bunch of alienkillers like me.

198747658.review:
This title marks the switch from X-Com games taking themselves somewhat seriously to absolute parodies.
As such, this isn't a classic X-Com game, story-wise. It's polished, it's pretty and it's fun, but it doesn't carry the atmosphere. 

Buy if you liked the TBS gameplay of X-Com: Enemy Unknown and if on sale.

- If you like proper storytelling and atmosphere of earlier X-Com titles, there are much better games out there.
- If you on the other hand like a *good* tactical TBS, there are also much better games out there.

Despite - maybe *because* - being a proper fan of the franchise ever since UFO, this does in no way scratch my itch.

198380895.review:
Gameplay Mechanics: 10/10
Narrative: 7/10
Graphics: 7/10
Sound and Music: 8/10
Replayability: 7/10
Innovation: 8/10

XCOM 2 is a masterclass in tactical turn-based combat. It builds on Enemy Unknown with smarter systems, new mechanics, and more brutal consequences—turning every mission into a white-knuckle test of nerves and decision-making. It’s one of the most polished tactical strategy games ever made.

Gameplay mechanics are flawless. From the concealment system to the action economy, flanking, overwatch traps, and class-based abilities—every element works together to make combat feel intense and rewarding. The game constantly challenges you to think ahead while adapting to chaos mid-mission. Score: 10/10.

Narrative is a solid backdrop. Taking place in a world where humanity already lost, the resistance theme works well and gives the campaign weight. It’s not a deeply personal story, but it sets a strong tone and includes enough cinematic moments to keep things engaging. Score: 7/10.

Graphics hold up well. Character models are customizable and detailed, environments are destructible and varied, and the action camera adds drama to your biggest hits (or misses). There are some rough animations and performance issues, but overall it looks great for a tactics game. Score: 7/10.

Sound and music do a great job of selling the tension. Pulse-pounding mission themes, satisfying weapon effects, and strong voice work make every encounter feel serious. Score: 8/10.

Replayability is good but not endless. Each run will differ slightly based on RNG and soldier progression, but the campaign structure is fairly fixed, and missions can start to feel repetitive. Mods and War of the Chosen definitely help, but the base experience has its limits. Score: 7/10.

Innovation is strong. The introduction of concealment, timed objectives, and a dynamic strategy layer gave the game a sharper edge. These systems genuinely evolve the genre. Score: 8/10.

Bottom line: XCOM 2 delivers pure tactical brilliance. Every decision counts, and the game doesn’t hold your hand. If you’re up for the challenge, it’s one of the most rewarding strategy games you’ll ever play.

198087954.review:
In a world overrun by aliens, failure isn’t a plot twist—it’s a game mechanic. XCOM® 2, released in 2016, took the already punishing brilliance of XCOM: Enemy Unknown and turned the pressure dial to 11. Nearly a decade later, it still holds its ground as one of the most tense, rewarding, and punishing tactical strategy games ever made.

If you’ve ever screamed at a 95% miss or mourned a veteran sniper lost to a Sectoid crit, you already know: XCOM 2 doesn’t play fair—but that’s exactly why we love it.

Unlike its predecessor, XCOM 2 begins with humanity already having lost. The alien overlords (ADVENT) have taken over Earth, and you're not defending the planet—you're leading a resistance from the shadows.

This narrative twist changes everything:
-Missions feel like guerrilla warfare, not defense ops.
-You’re outgunned and always on the back foot.
-The stakes feel higher because every decision is survival-based, not strategic luxury.

The result is a more aggressive, risk-taking kind of XCOM—where bold plays are often your only shot.

XCOM 2 retains the core elements that define the franchise:
-Turn-based tactical combat on grid-based maps
-Permadeath for soldiers, complete with fully customizable names, bios, and gear
-Resource juggling and tech progression on the global map (now a mobile command ship)

But it adds meaningful upgrades:
-Concealment mechanics that allow for ambushes and sneak tactics
-More diverse mission types, with timers, hostage rescues, and sabotage
-Modular weapon upgrades and personal soldier stories
-Procedurally generated maps, keeping each campaign fresh
-Expanded classes and skill trees, like the stealthy Ranger or explosive Grenadier

It’s not just a test of tactics—it’s a test of your nerve.

The strategic layer has you flying around the world in the Avenger, a stolen alien craft turned mobile HQ. You’ll:
-Choose where to allocate time and resources
-Make contact with resistance cells
-Prioritize R&D or construction
-Balance intel, supplies, and mission opportunities
-Counter the looming threat of the Avatar Project, the game’s ticking doomsday clock

The brilliance of XCOM 2 lies in resource scarcity. You’re never fully prepared. You’ll never feel ready. You must make hard choices constantly, and living with the consequences is the whole experience.

The 2017 War of the Chosen expansion elevates XCOM 2 from “excellent” to “genre-defining”:
-Introduces the Chosen, elite alien nemeses with unique abilities who hunt you across the campaign
-Adds three new resistance factions (Reapers, Skirmishers, Templars), each with new soldier classes and tactics
-Brings in bond mechanics between soldiers, fatigue systems, and covert actions
-It turns the game into a narrative-rich war saga—with rivalries, alliances, and emergent stories that rival some RPGs.

It’s so good that it’s hard to imagine XCOM 2 without it.

By 2025 standards, the visuals are showing their age—but art direction and atmosphere still deliver:
-Urban ruins, alien facilities, and jungle outposts are all distinct and memorable
-Cinematic camera angles during attacks heighten the drama (even when they miss...)
-Sound design is exceptional—tense music swells, alien screeches, and soldier shouts enhance immersion
-Voice acting, particularly from Bradford and the Chosen, adds surprising weight

Plus, customization options are top-tier—you’ll get attached to your soldiers, and losing them hurts.

Weaknesses and Rough Edges:
-Performance issues were a big problem at launch (now largely patched, but still worth noting)
-RNG frustration: 95% hit misses will haunt you—though this is more a feature than a flaw to many
-Long load times and occasional stutters, especially with mods
-Some late-game missions can feel drawn-out or repetitive

Also, it’s hard. Even on normal difficulty, XCOM 2 pulls no punches. But if you’re looking for a forgiving power fantasy… you’re in the wrong war.

XCOM® 2 is not just a strategy game—it’s a story machine, a stress test, and a celebration of tactical brilliance under pressure. Whether you’re executing the perfect ambush or watching your best soldier get crit from full cover, the game never stops delivering tension, triumph, and heartbreak.

With War of the Chosen included, it's easily one of the greatest turn-based tactics games ever made—and it still outclasses most of what the genre has offered in the years since.

Pros:
-Deep, rewarding tactical combat with real stakes
-Tons of customization and emergent storytelling
-Great variety of missions, enemies, and maps
-War of the Chosen expands everything dramatically
-Mod support keeps the game fresh for years

Cons:
-Occasional performance issues and bugs
-RNG can be brutal and demoralizing
-UI and info overload for newcomers
-Steep learning curve, especially without expansion

XCOM® 2 is a masterclass in strategy under pressure—a game where every victory feels earned, every loss stings, and every campaign writes a new war story. If you haven’t played it yet, don’t wait for the invasion to end.

Rating: 8/10

198022459.review:
TL;DR: It's a good turn-based combat strategy game. It does have some glaring issues, but it's definitely among the better ones out there. The EULA does seem needlessly invasive, but you can opt out, like I do every time I open the game, but if you do, you can't play multiplayer - I doubt anyone plays that.

The bad:
At times it does get stuck in especially overwatch-shot animations. The longest I have waited was 32 minutes, before it resolved itself. Frequent saves help if you're not in Ironman mode.

It will also act the same, if you do the same actions in the same order. Meaning that the same shots will miss, crit, and so forth, which I dislike.
It's a bit like the outcome has been predetermined before you start your mission.
If you move your unit to one spot and fire, they will miss, but if you move your unit to the spot next to the first one, they will crit.
The chance to hit the UI shows seems to be advisory. My units certainly hit or miss depending on the spot they stand on, even if they have the same chance to hit. The amount of 90%+ shots they miss is ridiculous.

It does have some odd RNG, I'm currently 5 years into a run aimed at getting all the achievements associated with the rulers, but I still haven't met the 3rd one, so I'm just waiting forever.

The good:
The world is reasonably well developed. The many adjustments you can make to units, team make up, and expanding in the world makes it easy to fit experience to your playstyle, making it much more enjoyable, although some units will certainly objectively be best, your not forced to use them.
For instance, I dislike Templars, although they're powerful. I just don't like them, so I rarely use them.

The graphics are decent quality, the same with the story, although I doubt either won any awards.

I have finally decided to write a review on this fantastic game after playing it for 950+ hours.  I probably hold a record.  I love this game, it's my world.  I have lost many times, and I have won many times, and I still keep playing it to win and lose many more times, I am truly the Commander.  

I love naming the soldiers after my friends and sending them off to die......I mean fight to free humanity.  It's funny when they get shot and get sent over the rainbow bridge or.....sad.  It's fun to see my friend get smashed by a Berserker Queen Monster that came out of no where.  

I love the theme of aliens and space.  I love the relationship building of the game.  I love the aliens in the game,  I don't like the Chosen Assassin though, she always able to hurt or maim at least someone.  I love XCOM's concept of psionics.  It's the most quantum and mystic of all concepts in video games.  

Another thing I do not like about this game is the sense of urgency, the doomsday clock as people call it.  That literally takes the fun out of it.  But I do like how they allow us to break the doomsday clock in the ini file.  That is very honourable of them, allowing people to enjoy the game on their own terms.  But I do like playing with the doomsday clock at times.  It literally makes the game that much harder.

Overall a fantastic game that I highly recommend others to play.  It's an old game now, but it's still very fun.  Creators of XCOM 2, if you read this...where is XCOM 3??  You just can't have us hanging like this, and XCOM Chimera Squad is not doing justice, it's fun, and I also recommend that game, but we need more.  It is time for XCOM 3.  You can't just leave the story hanging like that.  The next step must happen now, all of the elder's creations and slaves and maybe if any other Ethereals are still around must unite and finish off whatever was scaring the Ethereals.  I know there is something more scary out there, the Warlock said so himself I think.  Aliens and humans together fighting off the true God or Devil, would be siiiick.  

I highly recommend this game to everyone and their mom's and dad's and their kitchen sink, their refrigerator that keeps making strange sounds and their family dog, and their territorial cat.

197519552.review:
I played the vanilla game, and I thought it was pretty good. My review is mostly going to go over issues I had, but I did enjoy the game as a whole quite a lot, so know that while you read the rest.

Firstly, the bugs, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ there are wayyy too many bugs for a game that came out 8 years as of the time I played this, sometimes AOE attack particle effects don't show up so I have to load a save to be able to see where it will affect, sometimes the music just doesn't work and I have to restart the game, sometimes I'll target one enemy and for some reason, even though the indicator has a specific enemy selected, it will attack someone completely different, also, for some reason when I save some characters to the character pool, all appearance modifications get reset to what they were originally, and sometimes they don't, also if I lose access to a [spoiler]region after the resistance council guy dies, he still shows up and tells me I ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up[/spoiler]. I won't list any more, you get the idea, a lot of bugs, and it's weird that there are this many at this point in the game's life cycle.

What about the gameplay? It's pretty good. I did find the recurring missions to be much harder then the story missions, however, so that was a strange experience. For example, I would be doing a guerrilla op where my team comes back half dead and then I'll fight [spoiler]3 avatars at once, after like 30 other enemies[/spoiler] and I won't even get anyone gravely injured, the difficulty is very weird, I can only guess they made the campaign easier for people who are just looking to rush through the game, but if this is you I *strongly* do NOT recommend doing that because that would take like 90% of the fun out of the game.

I do wish that there was a bit more enemy variety, however. A lot of the enemies are very one-note, and once you get used to them, you don't really have to change up what you're doing very often. When they introduced the [spoiler]Sectopod and Chrysallid[/spoiler] enemies, that was probably when I had the most trouble adapting, but, again, there wasn't really much changing to my strategy I had to do, I just [spoiler]respec'd some of my grenadiers to be able to shred armor[/spoiler] and focused on taking them down first, there was just something missing that made it so that I did not have to change my squad composition or how I generally played very often. The timed missions make an attempt at doing this by rushing you to complete a task, but they don't accomplish this very well because I usually had plenty of time to position my squad how I liked and do whatever the task was in time.

I feel like I should go over the management gameplay as well, but there's not much to say. It's really more a time waster than a fun management system. You build rooms that do things. And then those rooms do things. That is a very vague and useless statement, but playing the game made me feel like that is an adequate descriptor of what it is. It's very dull, and I did not like it and it is boring. 

You do need to expand to be able to keep the [spoiler]Avatar project[/spoiler] bar low, but that doesn't really make it any more fun. I also wish there was more nuanced soldier management than just: they get injured sometimes, and then you have to wait until they're not. I guess they get [spoiler]"Shaken"[/spoiler] sometimes, but that's a very lazy and overly simple implementation of a PTSD mechanic. I know "War of the Chosen" adds some stuff in that domain, but the vanilla game is sorely lacking it.

In summary, the gameplay can be fun, but a bit repetitive once you've played it for a while. The story is nonsense, but not what you should be here for, and the game is very buggy. Also, the management system is boring.

Xcom 2: War of the Chosen Modded experience goes as follow:

-Doom Slayer and 2B become a deadly duo. He uses the Spnker from Halo 2 and a Spartan Laser, meanwhile she uses a Doom Eternal shotgun.
-Cortana the specialist hacker and Master Chief become a pair again. She uses a Covenant carbine, and he uses same loadout as Doom Slayer.

-Immediately added Thel'Vadam "The Arbiter" to the party.

The top-down combat is very fun, and leveling characters is very rewarding.
That is everything positive I will say about this game.
The random chance aspect is so upsetting that it's wrote to even complain about it, so I will just let you know it's as annoying as everyone says it is.
What's more is the time limits on this game.  The Avatar Project makes what should be an exciting and sandbox-y game feel constantly stressful and railroady, and when you add all the time-limited attacks on top, it just gets frustrating.  This goes doubly for the DLC; you can hardly finish one upgrade or training before some zombie ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ crops up and takes 40 minutes of your time.
The DLC stories also suck.  They juggle so many tonally conflicting and mutually boring plotlines that it just feels like the game is overloaded with junk.  If they had just stuck with the core elements a little longer and refined the gameplay, it would have been great, but instead we ended up with a big grab-bag of disparate elements, none of them polished enough to warrant being in the game.
Disappointing. :(

197407352.review:
I've had this game since maybe 2018, and bought it originally without the DLC. I think the base game is pretty good, perfectly serviceable for a turn-based RTS. The DLC is completely worth it, when talking about War of the Chosen. Legacy Pack, Shens Last Gift, and the other one (maybe?) are worth it if theyre on sale or you want some more flavor that fits the game. The modding community single-handedly keep me playing this game 7 years later. If you like turn-based RTS experiences with light base building, this is a must buy no doubt. 
I'd be remiss to not mention some downsides. Compared to Enemy Unknown, the base building doesnt feel as great as it used to. WotC and the resistance ring helps with this, as does Long War 2, an expansion mod that overhauls the game just like EU, but compared to it, it cant stand on its own feet too well. Also bugs are prevalent throughout the game all these years later, and many, MANY shots or actions you take will take ages to work. Some mods help with this, but a lot of people have gotten more used to the jank than anything. 
If the next game looks like this one, I'd be satisfied. If it looks like Chimera Squad, I'd be skeptical if not upset. If it looks like Enemy Unknown, it will be an honest to god day 1 premium preorder. Haven't done that since I got my hopes up on BF 2042. 
TL;DR: Buy this game if youre a single player strategy nerd who has hours to burn after work or duty. seriously.

197253767.review:
Playing this game for the first time made me realise that if you want to actually win, you got to think before you waste your turn. Can only see one pod of enemies? why not scout ahead a bit more to see if engaging is worth it. Some aliens are behind full cover? Use an explosive or flank em. These situations which the game gives you and allows you and ONLY you to handle feel amazing if you correctly pull it off. However, the gut wrenching feeling when your favourite character dies or when you miss a 90-99% shot is like no other. Only XCOM can punish you for making the right choice. It does bring the ever sense of dread and doom the game tries so hard to captivate you with. I do also enjoy the WOTC DLC, adding new enemies, classes and even bosses helps keep the game fresh and active. 

Overall, this has to be my top 5 if not top 1 turn based game with mods from the community adding even more depth to an already deep game and graphics which in my opinion still holds up solidly. I guess I will be playing this until XCOM 3 comes out 9/10

Decided to come back to the game...
Saw a lot of negative recent reviews...
---- "Small" rant ----
People complaining about recent EULA changes, so I decided to check.
They haven't changed XCOM 2's EULA for nearly than 6 years, since July 13, 2019 to be exact...at least on Steam.
As soon as you open the game, you have to accept the new one from February 2025, you can opt out, but then you can't log in for multiplayer......does anyone still play multiplayer?
Anyway, you list one EULA on the store, then a different one on the "product"...I don't think that's legal, even in US.
And the information they collect...I don't even provide half of it and they can't force me to, at least where I live.
Finally, if you're worried about arbitration, the 2019 version already restricts you to it.....why worry now?
Send them the opt-out letter sooner next time...
---- END ----
As for the game, it's a pretty solid XCOM title.
I find they have a little too much "timed" stuff, but that's my opinion.
Also, Steam Workshop mods and Nexus mods work just fine...

Take-Two 服务条款
最后更新日期：2025 年 2 月 28 日
Take-Two 单方面向一款已有 13 年历史的游戏添加了新的服务条款，其中规定使用模组将会被封号，同时还可以因为分享漏洞或利用手段而封禁你。虽然我怀疑他们会把这种权力用于《无主之地2》的玩家，但毫无疑问会应用在《无主之地4》上。Randy 曾称展示漏洞或模组的人为“网络恐怖分子”，而现在这家公司拥有权力可以将你从新老《无主之地》系列中封禁。
服务条款中的关键信息：
使用模组将被视为封禁行为
展示作弊行为或漏洞利用行为会被封禁
若你的地区法律允许，将强制仲裁解决争议
连接在线服务器时使用 VPN 可被封禁
无法在虚拟机上访问游戏内容
最重要的一点：公司保留在任何时间修改本协议全部或部分的权利
一家 CEO 曾被指控将含有非法内容的 USB 以及 Gearbox/2K 合作伙伴的敏感数据遗落在一家中世纪主题餐厅的公司，如今希望收集你所有可口的数据，并承诺无论有意或无意，都不会让其他人接触到这些数据。ᴾʳᵒᵐᶦˢᵉ（注：讽刺语气，意为“他们保证”）
收集的数据类型包括：
标识符 / 联系信息：姓名、用户名、游戏 ID、邮寄地址和电子邮箱、电话号码、唯一 ID、移动设备 ID、平台 ID、游戏平台服务 ID、广告 ID（如 IDFA、Android ID）以及 IP 地址
受保护特征：年龄和性别
商业信息：购买和使用记录、偏好设置，包括游戏数据
账单信息：支付信息（信用卡 / 借记卡信息）及收货地址
网络 / 电子活动：网站 / 应用的浏览记录以及与服务相关的游戏信息；你与服务或广告的在线互动信息；你使用的游戏和平台的详细信息，以及与已安装应用相关的其他信息
设备与使用数据：设备类型、软硬件详情、语言设置、浏览器类型及版本、操作系统，以及用户如何使用和互动服务的信息（例如：浏览内容、访问页面、点击、滚动等）
画像推断：通过你的信息和网络行为推断出个人画像，以帮助我们识别你可能感兴趣的商品和服务
音频 / 视觉信息：账户头像、图片和形象，通过聊天功能产生的音频信息，以及游戏录制和视频画面（如参与游戏测试时）
敏感信息：精确位置信息（若你允许服务收集定位数据）、账户凭证（用户名和密码）、以及通过聊天功能发送的通信内容

This is a good game with a lot of room for improvement. Everything about the gameplay feels tight, class design is great and difficulty feels fair, but the mission structure is too repetitive, the game introduces new weapons and enemies but not new mechanics, so doing the same type of world research and mission goals but with new enemies and weapons can get very stale.
