196620348.review:
Summary: XCOM 2 great, buy it 

Details: I played XCOM 2 after beating XCOM: enemy unknown and its DLC enemy within so I have already had experience with general tactics, playstyle, and have already learned the hard lesson of it does not matter how good that percentage is, you can still miss even if it is a 100% chance to hit that all Xcom veterans will respond with "That's XCOM, baby!" Additionally, I was already spoiled in knowledge about this game because I watched other people play before playing this game for myself. However, despite the spoilers I still have a tremendous amount of fun and continue to play to this play even though I have beaten the game at least three times that I recall (vanilla, WOTC+all DLC, and modded). I also often play on commander difficulty, no iron man (second hardest difficulty). Before I finish though I must mention the modding community which is incredibly large. This game currently has 10,206 mods listed on the steam workshop (which makes modding very easy) at this moment this review is being written and more are added every week. A lot of these mods have a pretty good quality and some are borderline amazing. The modding community itself is what is currently keeping this game alive to this day because as some people have mentioned the game can become stale after long enough time which is where mods come in. Whether you are someone that hates themselves and want suffering, someone that wants an easier time, or just have some bug fixes mods will help you. They tend to be highly configurable and some mods are even dedicated to making others more adjustable! Additionally, the community itself tends to be pretty chill and responds quickly to questions and offer tips. 

If you want to see some soldiers that have aim worse than a stormtroopers fighting some aliens in a guerilla style warfare or Master Chief, The Doomslayer, Titus the Space Marine (from Warhammer 40k), A Legion Titan (Titanfall 2), Raiden (from Metal Gear Rising Revengence), Ghost (from Call of Duty), Rusty (from Armored Core 6), and five other Spec Ops soldiers take the fight to Advent, the Flood, Zombies, various SCP MTFs, Bandits, Defectors, and more then you have chosen the right game.

196390878.review:
An Incoherent babble of tips in no particular order (for war of the chosen)

Dead hostiles don't get to do damage to you, prioritize OFFENSIVE skills on Soldiers and SPARK Units. The name of the game, especially on higher difficulties, is ending fights the same turn you start them. Defensive abilities is planning for failure, except Rangers. Slash is fun. Rangers are fun. Running up to smack a sectoid at max range and triggering 3 more pods of enemies during the run as your last action of the turn is in a word - not.

Go ONE pod at a time (Two at most, sometimes accidents happen and you trip a second one, its only *minorly* the end of the world if you trip 3 in the early game)

Target Priority is wonky as ♥♥♥♥, who do you target when given the choice between The Sectoid that likes to either mind control one of your guys/raise the dead? Maybe that Codex thing that teleports around the battlefield disabling weapons and cloning itself when shot? Or how about that Priest that throws around mind controls, stasis, and some buff called Holy Warrior? I mean the only other Viable Target is The Stun Lancer and he doesn't do anything special right?

Wrong, the stun lancer does the most special thing of all. Actually attacks you on his first turn and *reliably* does damage. TL;DR: In that hypothetical 4-man pod, The Lancer dies first because he Shoots/Charges round 1, followed by the Codex because it starts shooting on round 2, Psi Bomb is annoying but it only does damage if you stay inside the obvious purple death field. Then the Sectoid because unless it has a flank it almost never shoots on turn 1 and USUALLY doesn't shoot on round 2, its only its third turn and later that it starts attacking and as for the useless priest... It does a rotation of Stasis, Mind Control, Holy Warrior, then and only then, shoot. The order of its Psi powers varies a *little bit* depending on the situation, but the point is that he has so many "tricks" that it takes him forever to remember that he has a gun, and none of those tricks are "Do damage to player" Of the 3 titular "Chosen" bosses, the Assassin you will quickly note, is the hardest to deal with for the exact same reason you will come to loathe the existence of Stun Lancers above *most* other units in that she hurts you when given a turn, while the Warlock... Is trying his best, much like priests.

Speaking of The Chosen
Don't sleep on their weapons after you break their strongholds, The Warlock doesn't like touching his gun for some reason but I for one find his Disruptor Rifle to be a walking warcrime. Load with Bluescreen Rounds and watch every mechanical unit in the game cease to be an enemy you even acknowledge let alone fear.

of the resistance factions
Reapers have poor damage until they gain the banish ability, after this happens put a superior extended magazine on their vektor rifle and watch them turn into street magicians whose special power is to make one ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ of your choosing disappear before turning invisible themselves because their Stealth is pure ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ at all stages of the game, solid units despite initially low damage. Favor either Bluescreen or AP rounds depending on whether you want banish to GENERALLY kill whatever you point them at, or guarantee that sectopod only takes one action to reduce to its component parts.

Skirmishers trivialize most of the early game, they fall off late game but that's okay because by late game you can handle the heavier stuff and they still deal with the squishier aliens/advent just fine because of their action economy. Ignore Battlelord, it SOUNDS amazing, but it doesn't work as advertised because its horrendously bugged.

Templars take a bit to get used to as they spend their first few ranks basically being "Ranger but worse" but if you stick with them (and the correct Ranger Talents drop in their hidden tree... Looking at you Bladestorm and Reaper Mode) they have the *potential* to be the second most broken unit you can field. I mean the first is always going to be a Psi Operative holding the Disruptor Rifle but Templars can get *CLOSE* to mildly impressing them.

Oh wait this a recommendation... Yes. Yes I do.

I'm gonna make this short because I really wanna go back to playing it:

If you’re thinking of buying this, don’t buy it at full price. Games are always more fun when you get them when they’re on sale, and XCom 2 goes on sale pretty frequently for like 95% off. Seriously, get XCom 2 and all its dlc when it’s on sale, you’ll enjoy it so much more than if you bought it for full price. 

If you enjoy turn based strategy games, you HAVE to try XCom 2. If you played Wasteland 3 and loved it, you'll love XCom 2. 

For anyone reading this that has already played XCom 2 and are looking for similar games, try 'Wasteland 3' and 'Phoenix Point' (phoenix point has a free demo)

Edit: Alright, I’m back to give some more info. If you’re going to get all the dlc, I recommend you do 2 playthroughs (at least): one playthrough of the base game with all the dlc EXCEPT for War Of The Chosen (WOTC) and then a second playthrough with War Of The Chosen. Honestly, I’m doing my second playthrough right now, and I’ve started getting bored with it, but that’s probably because it’s the only game I’ve been playing. It took me over 60 hours to get bored with this game. I’ll 100% revisit this in half a year or a year.

196270864.review:
This game is why I will never set foot in a casino. Its addictive and the odds are stacked against you.

I figured after playing it through once and understanding the game it would be better the second time, but it doesn't matter how carefully you plan, or how well you setup and ambush there's nothing quite like setting up an ambush and having 5 of your guys miss 80%+ chances to hit in a row followed by them getting so destroyed that theres no recovery other than save scumming.

I dont want to have to save scum, but even on normal difficulty, unless you play perfectly you have to. Sometimes you play perfectly just for the game to spawn in 6 enemies from the shadows right at the end of your turn and then call in reinforcements on top of your position. So they all get to move and shoot and just annihilate you.

Aside from the setting, Mechanicus has improved on every single aspect of this game. Overwatch is actually visible, theres a clear turn order, the action bar doesnt change and update for each character after every move, theres no gambling on shots: if its in range and you have a clear shot you will it it unless they dodge.

Speaking of dodging, why the hell is that not included in the hit percentage... So I have a 80% chance my idiot can shoot in a straight line, and that my bullet will make it to the enemy, but what happens then is yet another calculation that I cant even see..? The amount of times I watched dozens of rounds fly directly through an enemy just for it to say grazed or dodged is just stupid.

Why can I make a full stack of armor and weapons for every soldier on the ship but I have to make individual medkits? Who thought that was a good idea?

I would like to see a 3rd installment of the game, where they use all the improvements that Mechanicus made to the formula, because again, I liked the setting.

196195645.review:
I like Xcom and with Xcom 2 many aspects of Xcom got better. Lots of customization, lots of really good mods. I also liked playing as an underdog rebel. With higher difficulty settings game cheats, but it's nothing too bad. No bases, just a spaceship that you cruise around the world contacting resistance cells and doing missions. No jets doing dog fighting with aliens either, but you really don't miss it while playing.

Dlc's are ok, nothing special. Except Monster Hunter, that is really bad one. Adds few missions, unique items and randomly spawning unique boss monsters that can spawn in any mission. Picture this: you are having a tight game with a evac window of 8 turns and three squaties and one rookie. Lots of aliens, so you set up an ambush while concealed. Suddenly, there is a white boss snake, that can move and attack multiple times in a turn, so it kills two of your guys with one turn and the rookie panics. In next turn, all of the troops are dead, ambush is ruined and mission is ruined. You go to next mission, and the same repeats. Even with easier difficulty. 

I managed to play game through with monster hunter just once and it wasn't fun. Atleast until the bosses were slain. Skip it if you can.

War of the chosen was good addon. More customization on troops, new skills, new game mechanics, new missions, and characters. I even found myself making after-mission propaganda posters. First I though that it was a dumb idea, but it turned out fun. The three assassins were easy, generic and their dialog was really bad. They were defeated quickly and easily. I've had tighter battles with an Andomedan, than with any of these "elite fighters". I think, the dlc would have been just fine without them.

So, other than lazy, cringe worthy writing and easily defeatable assassins, The War of the Chosen is worth of playing through.

I can recommend Xcom 2.

195830222.review:
XCOM 2 base game - Great.
XCOM 2 w/ War of the Chosen DLC - One of the best games someone can experience. 
XCOM 2 WOTC w/ mods - You didn't think it could get any better, & yet it did. 

This is genuinely one of the best games ever made. You can play over & over again with no two campaigns ever being the same. When you throw a ton of mods on top of this, from cosmetics to gameplay additions, the replayability becomes unthinkable. You will experience the highest of highs, & the lowest of lows. You might be brought so low that the campaign is a failure with no chance of saving. Yet you'll keep coming back, because you know you can beat it! 

The gameplay is intense & rewarding. Sometimes anger inducing, but that's part of the experience. 
Graphics are very nice, with a very distinctive art style that holds up very well. 
Music doesn't get talked about enough but is insanely good (would recommend using the soundtrack mixer mod that allows base XCOM 2, WOTC & Tactical Legacy soundtracks to all play together rather than only choosing one). 

TLDR - If you haven't played this game yet, what the hell are you waiting for?! Get to it, Commander!

195564728.review:
I'm not usually a fan of turn-based RPG's due to their inherently slow pacing. 
However, XCOM and XCOM 2 somehow manages to combine both the action and strategic depth. 

You'll be able to play, enjoy and understand the story and the setting of XCOM 2 without having played the first game, although the first game would help you understand a little bit more of the background for some of the characters in XCOM 2. 
If you liked the first XCOM, you're going to love XCOM 2, especially with its War of the Chosen expansion. Although, maybe do a playtrough with the vanilla version of the game first, if you're new to the franchise, because the expansion adds a lot of new elements. 

I have to admit that I prefered the atmosphere of the first XCOM game when I first started XCOM 2, because XCOM 2 at first glance looked too cartoonish and clean. But I soon realized that only the enemies looked that way, that there was a reason, and it was only at the very beggining. XCOM 2 is at least every bit as gritty and realistic as the first game was. 

Finally, just know that you will be frustrated and annoyed with the games hit-chance mechanics, sometimes unbelievable misses and you might be punished for rushing. But once you get the hang of it, and put a little thought behind your approach, it'll bring you both a lot of joy and succes. 
And remember, you can always pull back a little, retreat to gain a tactical advantage, and lure the enemy into a bad position. There's no need to always mindlessly push forward. 

Good luck on the battlefield, soldier.

195441435.review:
XCOM 2 is not only one of the finest turn-based tactics games ever made, it deserves a place in the pantheon of all-time strategy greats. Firaxis’ first effort to remaster 90s classic X-COM was a solid game in its own right, but also deserves recognition for reinvigorating the turn-based tactics genre. Its success back in 2012 ushered in a wave of accomplished descendants. Yet in XCOM’s 2016 sequel, Firaxis have set the bar fearsomely high for the competitors they helped create. XCOM 2 takes everything good in the first game and polishes it to a mirror sheen, while reinventing or ditching a few elements that didn’t quite work. The result is a compelling masterclass in squad-based tactics. 

As in the original, XCOM 2 sees you assemble fresh-faced recruits, train and equip them in the strategic overworld layer, and advance your mobile base’s capabilities. The meat of the game, however, is found in turn-based battles between your squishy soldiers and endless squads of enemies. The early game remains ferociously challenging, with a fair chance that irreplaceable soldiers will be one-shotted by a lucky adversary. That, as they say, is XCOM, baybee. As your squad gains more skills and better equipment – not least the borderline overpowered Mimic Beacons – the late game becomes more forgiving. Compared with the first game, hit percentages seem to have been tweaked to be both more reasonable and more transparent. You’ll still be praying to the RNG quite a bit, though the uncertainty is needed to prevent decision-paralysis and introduce a welcome sense of chaos to what could be polite turn-taking. XCOM is at its best when the tension is high. Once you’ve wrapped your head around the basic flow of the campaign, it really is best to grit your teeth and dive into Iron Man on higher difficulties. Timed missions are more frequent in this installment but add welcome variety. Though one could wish for a richer and more dynamic strategic layer, the interaction between the micro and macro remains fundamentally delicious. XCOM 2’s feel is noticeably improved over its elder brother, with chunkier explosions, more detailed soldier customisation, smoother transitions and a host of other refinements that add up to a slick and enticing package. 

XCOM 2 reimagines the alien invasion of the first game as a fait accompli, and recasts your band of soldiers as plucky resistance warriors against the totalitarian “Advent” regime. Though I’ve seen some pine for the original game’s premise of XCOM as a shadowy government appendage battling a largely unseen foe, I personally prefer the scrappier feel of the sequel’s fiction. Notwithstanding Advent’s apparent inability to track a flying aircraft carrier around the globe or guard its critical infrastructure with more than a dozen guys, for me the absurd premise hangs together better as an underdog guerilla war. Firaxis take a page from Valve’s playbook and recognise that the only viable future for humanity following a curb-stomp by a hostile interstellar civilisation would be xeno-colonialism. The world-building in XCOM 2 seems significantly stronger than the first game’s somewhat generic military sci-fi, with some fun riffs on jackbooted alien overlords and the iconography of rebellion. Your squad will battle through gleaming Advent techno-metropolises, dilapidated rural outposts and sinister alien processing facilities that show, rather than tell, the consequences of Advent dominion. The rather thrown-together, junkyard vibe of your operation feels great pitted against the aliens’ uncanny perfection. It’s perhaps just a shame that the designers couldn’t resist the same impulse as in the first game to transform your soldiers into Power Rangers with the final round of tech upgrades. The writing, however, remains Hollywood-dumb, far short of Firaxis’ earlier industry-leading work on their 1999 masterpiece Alpha Centauri. While the plot of both XCOMs is essentially a MacGuffin hunt that culminates in a silly showdown, the sequel is enlivened by a likeable cast bickering, speculating on next steps and pining for “Advent burgers.” The stakes and the aliens’ intentions are also clearer, with the doomsday tracker and your foe’s counter-moves giving the campaign a more adversarial feel than the original’s rather vague rising tide of panic.

Before wrapping this review up, it’s worth considering XCOM 2’s massive War of the Chosen DLC, which offers a host of gameplay refinements and a somewhat-different tone. Is it essential? No, the base game is hugely compelling without it, and some players who own WOTC will still choose to play an unadulterated campaign for its more focused mechanics and less MCU-flavoured fiction. If you have a good time with vanilla XCOM 2 and are hungry for more, however, WOTC introduces a generous seasoning of new biomes, scenarios, enemy types and mechanical complexity. These are all welcome, and add great replayability. Where WOTC can be more divisive is in “hero” units, recurring supervillain antagonists, and additional options to power up your soldiers. This takes an already somewhat-silly game into even more cartoonish territory. It’s still tough, and mostly grounded, but accelerates XCOM’s tendency for both failure and success to snowball, pushing the game out of its difficulty sweet spot. Unforgivably, WOTC does nothing to address the base game’s tendency for the later campaign to be a cakewalk: indeed, it tends to accelerate it. It’s a missed opportunity to reinvigorate the late game, where your operation’s hard-won strength can paradoxically sap missions of their tension. 

As a final aside, it’s worth mentioning that publisher 2K retroactively blighted XCOM 2 in 2022 with a useless mandatory launcher, along with a number of their other titles (though not, curiously, the original XCOM). This led to review-bombing that I personally think is a little unfair. Happily, 2K removed the launcher in 2025, but you’ll still see the effect in the review scores here.

In the final analysis, XCOM 2 stands tall as one of Firaxis’ finest gameplay achievements, elevated by a superlative atmosphere and a hundred thoughtful touches. There’s more to commend here, from the excellent score to the dynamic stories that emerge from your troops being thrown into the fray again and again, but you’ll discover all that for yourself. The narrative, and especially War of the Chosen’s drift into superhero territory, wasn’t to my taste, but that’s easy to overlook when every other component of the game is so strong. XCOM 2 is a classic, and highly recommended. The original still holds up, and is different enough, to also be worth checking out, but if you have room in your schedule for only one turn-based tactics battler, make it this one.

195377315.review:
I believe XCOM 2 is a fantastic game but it's just so unfortunate that it might not for me.

The accuracy system is extremely mentally draining to deal with, I'm not talking about the result - it's randomness, which I understand. I'm talking about the process of planning around those randomness.

40% hit rate, do I take the shot or not? How about 70%? How about 85%? Can I afford to miss in the unfortunate 15%? This cover reduce 20% enemy hit rate is it worth to use one action to go there? I have heard about the frustration of hit rate in XCOM but it was worse than I expected. Maybe it's just me but I feel bad to hit a 40% shot instead of feeling lucky - yeah all that planning going in my brain is not better than just rolling the dice.

Yeah save-scumming exists but moral issues aside, the load speed is not the best so it's not a good experience either.

I don't know why not making every hit always deals the expected amount of damage (hit rate x damage) every time, yeah it makes the game much easier but isn't that just more fun on average?
